nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:algebra-of-lipschitz-functions]
{Algebra of Lipschitz Functions}}{%
  Algebra of Lipschitz Functions}
nodes.question:
Which algebraic operations preserve the Lipschitz condition,
  and with what constants?
nodes.statement_display:
Always: $cf$ (constant $|c|K_f$), $f \pm g$ (constant
  $K_f+K_g$), $h \circ f$ (constant $K_hK_f$). With boundedness:
  $fg$ (constant $M_gK_f + M_fK_g$), $f/g$ when $|g| \geq k > 0$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Always: $cf$ (constant $|c|K_f$), $f \pm g$ (constant
  $K_f+K_g$), $h \circ f$ (constant $K_hK_f$). With boundedness:
  $fg$ (constant $M_gK_f + M_fK_g$), $f/g$ when $|g| \geq k > 0$.
nodes.hint:
Composition is always clean ($K_hK_f$, multiply the
  constants). Products need the add-and-subtract trick, which
  introduces $|f|$ and $|g|$ as coefficients.
nodes.negation_display:
$f = g = \mathrm{id}$ on $\mathbb{R}$: both Lipschitz-1,
  but $fg = x^2$ is not Lipschitz (slope $2x$ unbounded).
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  (a) $|cf(x)-cf(u)| = |c|\,|f(x)-f(u)| \leq |c|K_f|x-u|$. \\
  (b) Triangle inequality: $|(f+g)(x)-(f+g)(u)| \leq (K_f+K_g)|x-u|$. \\
  (c) TODO: chain $K_hK_f$. \\
  (d) TODO: add-subtract; bound by $M_gK_f + M_fK_g$. \\
  (e) TODO: show $1/g$ Lip with $K_g/k^2$; apply (d).

statement.plain:
Always: $cf$ (constant $|c|K_f$), $f \pm g$ (constant
  $K_f+K_g$), $h \circ f$ (constant $K_hK_f$). With boundedness:
  $fg$ (constant $M_gK_f + M_fK_g$), $f/g$ when $|g| \geq k > 0$.

negation.plain:
$f = g = \mathrm{id}$ on $\mathbb{R}$: both Lipschitz-1,
  but $fg = x^2$ is not Lipschitz (slope $2x$ unbounded).

flash_q.plain:
Which algebraic operations preserve the Lipschitz condition,
  and with what constants?

flash_a.plain:
Always: $cf$ (constant $|c|K_f$), $f \pm g$ (constant
  $K_f+K_g$), $h \circ f$ (constant $K_hK_f$). With boundedness:
  $fg$ (constant $M_gK_f + M_fK_g$), $f/g$ when $|g| \geq k > 0$.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:algebra-of-uniform-continuity-bounded]
{Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a Bounded Domain}}{%
  Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a Bounded Domain}
nodes.question:
Which algebraic operations preserve uniform continuity on
  a \emph{bounded} domain?
nodes.statement_display:
All of: $f+g$, $f-g$, $cf$, and $fg$. Also $f/g$ if $g$
  is bounded away from zero. Boundedness of the domain forces $f$
  and $g$ to be bounded, which controls the cross-terms in the
  product estimate.
nodes.statement_tex:
All of: $f+g$, $f-g$, $cf$, and $fg$. Also $f/g$ if $g$
  is bounded away from zero. Boundedness of the domain forces $f$
  and $g$ to be bounded, which controls the cross-terms in the
  product estimate.
nodes.hint:
Key step: UC on bounded set $\Rightarrow$ bounded image.
  Then $|(fg)(x)-(fg)(u)| \leq M_f|g(x)-g(u)| + M_g|f(x)-f(u)|$
  and both terms are controlled.
nodes.negation_display:
Product fails on unbounded domain: $x \cdot x = x^2$ not
  UC on $\mathbb{R}$.

statement.plain:
All of: $f+g$, $f-g$, $cf$, and $fg$. Also $f/g$ if $g$
  is bounded away from zero. Boundedness of the domain forces $f$
  and $g$ to be bounded, which controls the cross-terms in the
  product estimate.

negation.plain:

  UC on $\mathbb{R}$.

flash_a.plain:

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:algebra-of-uniform-continuity-general]
{Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a General Domain}}{%
  Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a General Domain}
nodes.question:
Which algebraic operations preserve uniform continuity on
  a general domain?
nodes.statement_display:
$f+g$, $f-g$, and $cf$ are UC whenever $f$ and $g$ are.
  Products fail in general: $x \cdot x = x^2$ is not UC on
  $\mathbb{R}$ even though $x$ is.
nodes.statement_tex:
$f+g$, $f-g$, and $cf$ are UC whenever $f$ and $g$ are.
  Products fail in general: $x \cdot x = x^2$ is not UC on
  $\mathbb{R}$ even though $x$ is.
nodes.hint:
$\delta = \min(\delta_f, \delta_g)$ works for sums and
  differences. Products need a bound on $f$ and $g$ to control the
  cross-terms.

statement.plain:
$f+g$, $f-g$, and $cf$ are UC whenever $f$ and $g$ are.
  Products fail in general: $x \cdot x = x^2$ is not UC on
  $\mathbb{R}$ even though $x$ is.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:bilipschitz-inverse-is-lipschitz]
{Inverse of a Bi-Lipschitz Map Is Lipschitz}}{%
  Inverse of a Bi-Lipschitz Map Is Lipschitz}
nodes.question:
If $f$ is bi-Lipschitz with bounds $\alpha$ and $K$, what
  can be said about $f^{-1}$?
nodes.statement_display:
$f^{-1}$ exists and is bi-Lipschitz with bounds $1/K$
  and $1/\alpha$. In particular $f^{-1}$ is Lipschitz with
  constant $1/\alpha$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$f^{-1}$ exists and is bi-Lipschitz with bounds $1/K$
  and $1/\alpha$. In particular $f^{-1}$ is Lipschitz with
  constant $1/\alpha$.
nodes.hint:
Substitute $y=f(x)$, $v=f(u)$ and divide the lower
  Lipschitz bound of $f$ by $\alpha$.
nodes.negation_display:
$x^3$ on $[-1,1]$: Lipschitz but not bi-Lipschitz (lower
  bound fails at $0$), so $x^{1/3}$ is not Lipschitz.
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. Injectivity: lower bound forces $x \neq u \Rightarrow f(x) \neq f(u)$. \\
  2. Set $y = f(x)$, $v = f(u)$. Lower bound on $f$:
     $\alpha|f^{-1}(y)-f^{-1}(v)| \leq |y-v|$;
     divide by $\alpha$ to get Lipschitz constant $1/\alpha$ for $f^{-1}$. \\
  3. Upper bound on $f$: $|y-v| \leq K|f^{-1}(y)-f^{-1}(v)|$;
     divide by $K$ to get lower bound $1/K$ for $f^{-1}$.

statement.plain:
$f^{-1}$ exists and is bi-Lipschitz with bounds $1/K$
  and $1/\alpha$. In particular $f^{-1}$ is Lipschitz with
  constant $1/\alpha$.

negation.plain:
$x^3$ on $[-1,1]$: Lipschitz but not bi-Lipschitz (lower
  bound fails at $0$), so $x^{1/3}$ is not Lipschitz.

flash_q.plain:
If $f$ is bi-Lipschitz with bounds $\alpha$ and $K$, what
  can be said about $f^{-1}$?

flash_a.plain:
$f^{-1}$ exists and is bi-Lipschitz with bounds $1/K$
  and $1/\alpha$. In particular $f^{-1}$ is Lipschitz with
  constant $1/\alpha$.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:cauchy-sequences-preserved]
{Uniform Continuity Preserves Cauchy Sequences}}{%
  Uniform Continuity Preserves Cauchy Sequences}
nodes.question: What does uniform continuity do to Cauchy sequences?
nodes.statement_display: Uniform continuity maps Cauchy sequences to Cauchy sequences.
nodes.statement_tex: Uniform continuity maps Cauchy sequences to Cauchy sequences.
nodes.hint:
One $\varepsilon$ drives both steps: uniform continuity
  converts $\varepsilon$ to $\delta$; the Cauchy condition converts
  $\delta$ to $N$.
nodes.formal:
$f$ uniformly continuous on $S$,\; $(x_n) \subseteq S$ Cauchy
$\;\Rightarrow\;$ $(f(x_n))$ Cauchy.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$f$ uniformly continuous on $S$,\; $(x_n) \subseteq S$ Cauchy
$\;\Rightarrow\;$ $(f(x_n))$ Cauchy.

statement.plain: Uniform continuity maps Cauchy sequences to Cauchy sequences.

formal.plain:
$f$ uniformly continuous on $S$,\; $(x_n) \subseteq S$ Cauchy
$\;\Rightarrow\;$ $(f(x_n))$ Cauchy.

flash_q.plain: What does uniform continuity do to Cauchy sequences?

flash_a.plain: Uniform continuity maps Cauchy sequences to Cauchy sequences.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:continuity-three-equiv]
{%
    Three Equivalent Forms of Continuity}}{%
    Three Equivalent Forms of Continuity}
nodes.question:
State the three equivalent forms of continuity of $f$
  at $c \in A$.
nodes.statement_display:
(i) $\varepsilon$-$\delta$: $\forall\varepsilon>0\,
  \exists\delta>0\,\forall x\in A:\,|x-c|<\delta\Rightarrow
  |f(x)-f(c)|<\varepsilon$.
  (ii) Neighbourhood: $f(A\cap V_\delta(c))\subseteq
  V_\varepsilon(f(c))$.
  (iii) Sequential: $x_n\to c\Rightarrow f(x_n)\to f(c)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
(i) $\varepsilon$-$\delta$: $\forall\varepsilon>0\,
  \exists\delta>0\,\forall x\in A:\,|x-c|<\delta\Rightarrow
  |f(x)-f(c)|<\varepsilon$.
  (ii) Neighbourhood: $f(A\cap V_\delta(c))\subseteq
  V_\varepsilon(f(c))$.
  (iii) Sequential: $x_n\to c\Rightarrow f(x_n)\to f(c)$.
nodes.hint:
(i)$\iff$(ii) is notation only.
  (i)$\iff$(iii) is the continuity analogue of Bartle 4.1.8,
  but sequences may now include $x_n = c$.
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. (i)$\Rightarrow$(ii): Fix $\varepsilon_0$, get $\delta_0$
from (i). Any $x_0 \in A \cap V_{\delta_0}(c)$ satisfies
$f(x_0) \in V_{\varepsilon_0}(f(c))$; arbitrariness gives the inclusion.
2. (ii)$\Rightarrow$(iii): Fix $\varepsilon$, get $\delta$ from (ii).
Sequential characterisation gives $N$ with $x_n \in V_\delta(c)$ for
$n \geq N$; neighbourhood condition pulls $f(x_n) \in V_\varepsilon(f(c))$.
3. (iii)$\Rightarrow$(i): Contrapositive. Failure of (i) at $\varepsilon_0$
yields $(x_n)$ via $\delta = 1/n$ with $x_n \to c$ but
$f(x_n) \not\to f(c)$, contradicting~(iii).

statement.plain:
(i) $\varepsilon$-$\delta$: $\forall\varepsilon>0\,
  \exists\delta>0\,\forall x\in A:\,|x-c|<\delta\Rightarrow
  |f(x)-f(c)|<\varepsilon$.
  (ii) Neighbourhood: $f(A\cap V_\delta(c))\subseteq
  V_\varepsilon(f(c))$.
  (iii) Sequential: $x_n\to c\Rightarrow f(x_n)\to f(c)$.

flash_q.plain:
State the three equivalent forms of continuity of $f$
  at $c \in A$.

flash_a.plain:
(i) $\varepsilon$-$\delta$: $\forall\varepsilon>0\,
  \exists\delta>0\,\forall x\in A:\,|x-c|<\delta\Rightarrow
  |f(x)-f(c)|<\varepsilon$.
  (ii) Neighbourhood: $f(A\cap V_\delta(c))\subseteq
  V_\varepsilon(f(c))$.
  (iii) Sequential: $x_n\to c\Rightarrow f(x_n)\to f(c)$.

nodes.question: When is a continuous function on $[a,b]$ injective?
nodes.statement_display:
Exactly when it is strictly monotone. Continuity plus
  injectivity forces strict monotonicity; conversely strict
  monotonicity implies injectivity.
nodes.statement_tex:
Exactly when it is strictly monotone. Continuity plus
  injectivity forces strict monotonicity; conversely strict
  monotonicity implies injectivity.
nodes.hint:
Non-monotonicity forces a local extremum; IVT then
  produces two preimages of some value, contradicting injectivity.

statement.plain:
Exactly when it is strictly monotone. Continuity plus
  injectivity forces strict monotonicity; conversely strict
  monotonicity implies injectivity.

flash_q.plain: When is a continuous function on $[a,b]$ injective?

flash_a.plain:
Exactly when it is strictly monotone. Continuity plus
  injectivity forces strict monotonicity; conversely strict
  monotonicity implies injectivity.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:continuous-inverse-theorem]
{Continuous Inverse Theorem}}{%
  Continuous Inverse Theorem}
nodes.question: When is the inverse of a function continuous?
nodes.statement_display:
If $f$ is strictly monotone and continuous on an interval $I$,
  then $f^{-1}$ is strictly monotone and continuous on $f(I)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
If $f$ is strictly monotone and continuous on an interval $I$,
  then $f^{-1}$ is strictly monotone and continuous on $f(I)$.
nodes.hint:
Continuity of $f$ + Preservation of Intervals ensures
  $f(I)$ is an interval with no gaps; a jump in $f^{-1}$ would
  create a gap in $f(I)$.

statement.plain:
If $f$ is strictly monotone and continuous on an interval $I$,
  then $f^{-1}$ is strictly monotone and continuous on $f(I)$.

flash_q.plain: When is the inverse of a function continuous?

flash_a.plain:
If $f$ is strictly monotone and continuous on an interval $I$,
  then $f^{-1}$ is strictly monotone and continuous on $f(I)$.

nodes.question: When is a monotone function on $[a,b]$ continuous?
nodes.statement_display:
Exactly when $f([a,b])$ is the closed interval $[f(a),f(b)]$
  (or $[f(b),f(a)]$ if decreasing) — no gaps in the range.
nodes.statement_tex:
Exactly when $f([a,b])$ is the closed interval $[f(a),f(b)]$
  (or $[f(b),f(a)]$ if decreasing) — no gaps in the range.
nodes.hint:
Each jump discontinuity creates a gap; no gaps means
  no jumps means continuous.

statement.plain:
Exactly when $f([a,b])$ is the closed interval $[f(a),f(b)]$
  (or $[f(b),f(a)]$ if decreasing) — no gaps in the range.

flash_a.plain:

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:global-continuity-theorem]
{Global Continuity Theorem}}{%
  Global Continuity Theorem}
nodes.question: State the Global Continuity Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
$f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous iff preimages of
  closed sets are closed, iff preimages of open sets are open.
nodes.statement_tex:
$f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous iff preimages of
  closed sets are closed, iff preimages of open sets are open.
nodes.hint:
Continuity = preimage preserves open/closed structure.
  This is the topological definition of continuity.

statement.plain:
$f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous iff preimages of
  closed sets are closed, iff preimages of open sets are open.

flash_a.plain:
$f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous iff preimages of
  closed sets are closed, iff preimages of open sets are open.

nodes.question:
If $f$ is continuous and injective on an interval, what
  can be said?
nodes.statement_display:
$f$ must be strictly monotone — either strictly increasing
  or strictly decreasing throughout.

$f$ must be strictly monotone — either strictly increasing
  or strictly decreasing throughout.

Non-monotonicity forces a local max or min, which IVT
  turns into a repeated value, contradicting injectivity.

statement.plain:

  or strictly decreasing throughout.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:inverse-function-theorem-monotone]
{Inverse Function Theorem for Continuous Strictly Monotone Functions}}{%
  Inverse Function Theorem for Continuous Strictly Monotone Functions}
nodes.question:
State the Inverse Function Theorem for continuous strictly
  monotone functions on $[a,b]$.
nodes.statement_display:
$f$ strictly monotone and continuous on $[a,b]$ implies
  $f([a,b]) = [f(a),f(b)]$ and $f^{-1}$ is continuous and strictly
  monotone on $f([a,b])$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$f$ strictly monotone and continuous on $[a,b]$ implies
  $f([a,b]) = [f(a),f(b)]$ and $f^{-1}$ is continuous and strictly
  monotone on $f([a,b])$.
nodes.hint:
Three conclusions: (1) inverse exists; (2) image is
  a closed interval; (3) inverse is continuous.

statement.plain:
$f$ strictly monotone and continuous on $[a,b]$ implies
  $f([a,b]) = [f(a),f(b)]$ and $f^{-1}$ is continuous and strictly
  monotone on $f([a,b])$.

nodes.question:
If $f$ is strictly monotone on $X$, what can be said about
  $f^{-1}$?
nodes.statement_display:
$f^{-1}$ exists on $f(X)$ and has the same monotonicity type
  as $f$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$f^{-1}$ exists on $f(X)$ and has the same monotonicity type
  as $f$.
nodes.hint:
Strict monotonicity gives injectivity; the inverse then
  inherits the order direction.
nodes.formal:
$f$ strictly increasing on $X$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$f^{-1}$ strictly increasing on $f(X)$.
Similarly for strictly decreasing.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$f$ strictly increasing on $X$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$f^{-1}$ strictly increasing on $f(X)$.
Similarly for strictly decreasing.

statement.plain:

  as $f$.

formal.plain:

$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$f^{-1}$ strictly increasing on $f(X)$.
Similarly for strictly decreasing.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:jump-intervals-for-monotone-discontinuities]
{Jump Intervals of a Monotone Function Are Disjoint}}{%
  Jump Intervals of a Monotone Function Are Disjoint}
nodes.question:
What is the jump interval at a discontinuity of a monotone
  function, and why are they disjoint?
nodes.statement_display:
The jump interval at $a$ is $(f(a^-), f(a^+))$ — the gap
  in the range. Distinct discontinuities produce disjoint jump
  intervals because monotonicity forces the ranges to be ordered.
nodes.statement_tex:
The jump interval at $a$ is $(f(a^-), f(a^+))$ — the gap
  in the range. Distinct discontinuities produce disjoint jump
  intervals because monotonicity forces the ranges to be ordered.
nodes.hint:
Monotonicity separates the jump intervals: $a < b$
  forces the jump at $a$ below the jump at $b$ with no overlap.
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. WLOG $f$ increasing, $c < d$. 2. For any $x \in (c,d)$:
$f(c^+) \leq f(x) \leq f(d^-)$ by monotonicity. 3. Hence $f(c^+) \leq f(d^-)$.
4. Every element of $J_c(f)$ is $< f(c^+)$ and every element of $J_d(f)$ is
$> f(d^-)$, so the intervals are disjoint.

statement.plain:
The jump interval at $a$ is $(f(a^-), f(a^+))$ — the gap
  in the range. Distinct discontinuities produce disjoint jump
  intervals because monotonicity forces the ranges to be ordered.

flash_q.plain:
What is the jump interval at a discontinuity of a monotone
  function, and why are they disjoint?

flash_a.plain:
The jump interval at $a$ is $(f(a^-), f(a^+))$ — the gap
  in the range. Distinct discontinuities produce disjoint jump
  intervals because monotonicity forces the ranges to be ordered.

  of a function?
nodes.statement_display:

  always.
nodes.hint:
$\sup \geq \inf$ on any set; take the inf/sup over
  $\delta$ and the inequality is preserved.

  \hyperref[prf:limsup-liminf-limit-criterion]
{Limit Exists iff Lim Sup Equals Lim Inf}}{%

nodes.question:
When does $\lim_{x \to x_0} f(x)$ exist in terms of
  $\limsup$ and $\liminf$?
nodes.statement_display:
Exactly when $\limsup_{x \to x_0} f(x) = \liminf_{x \to x_0}
  f(x) \in \mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Exactly when $\limsup_{x \to x_0} f(x) = \liminf_{x \to x_0}
  f(x) \in \mathbb{R}$.
nodes.hint:
The limit squeezes the upper and lower envelopes to the
  same finite value. If they disagree or diverge, no limit exists.
nodes.negation_display:
$\sin(1/x)$ at $0$: $\limsup = 1 \neq -1 = \liminf$, so
  no limit.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:lipschitz-special-functions]
{Special Lipschitz Functions}}{%
  Special Lipschitz Functions}
nodes.question: Which standard functions are Lipschitz and with what constants?
nodes.hint:
$|x|$ and $x^+$ use the reverse triangle inequality.
  $\max$/$\min$ use the identity $\max(f,g) = (f+g+|f-g|)/2$ or
  the direct estimate $|\max(f,g)(x)-\max(f,g)(u)| \leq
  \max(|f(x)-f(u)|,|g(x)-g(u)|)$.
nodes.negation_display:
$\sqrt{x}$ on $[0,1]$: UC but not Lipschitz. Slope
  $1/(2\sqrt{x}) \to \infty$ as $x \to 0^+$, so no $K$ works.

negation.plain:

  $1/(2\sqrt{x}) \to \infty$ as $x \to 0^+$, so no $K$ works.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:local-properties-of-continuous-functions]
{Local Properties of Continuous Functions}}{%
  Local Properties of Continuous Functions}
nodes.question: What four local properties does continuity at a point guarantee?
nodes.statement_display:
(1) Local boundedness near $a$. (2) Sign stability near $a$
  when $f(a)\neq 0$. (3) Algebra: sums, differences, products,
  quotients (when denominator $\neq 0$) are continuous at $a$.
  (4) Composition with a function continuous at $f(a)$ is continuous
  at $a$.
nodes.statement_tex:
(1) Local boundedness near $a$. (2) Sign stability near $a$
  when $f(a)\neq 0$. (3) Algebra: sums, differences, products,
  quotients (when denominator $\neq 0$) are continuous at $a$.
  (4) Composition with a function continuous at $f(a)$ is continuous
  at $a$.
nodes.hint:
Zorich bundles four separate results into one theorem.
  Each part corresponds to a theorem elsewhere in the notes.
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. Continuity at $c$ gives $\lim_{x \to c}f(x) = f(c)$. \\
  2. Part (1): cite Limit Implies Local Bounding. \\
  3. Part (2): cite Limits Bounded Away from Zero (needs $f(c) \neq 0$). \\
  4. Part (3): cite Sign Preservation.

statement.plain:
(1) Local boundedness near $a$. (2) Sign stability near $a$
  when $f(a)\neq 0$. (3) Algebra: sums, differences, products,
  quotients (when denominator $\neq 0$) are continuous at $a$.
  (4) Composition with a function continuous at $f(a)$ is continuous
  at $a$.

flash_q.plain: What four local properties does continuity at a point guarantee?

flash_a.plain:
(1) Local boundedness near $a$. (2) Sign stability near $a$
  when $f(a)\neq 0$. (3) Algebra: sums, differences, products,
  quotients (when denominator $\neq 0$) are continuous at $a$.
  (4) Composition with a function continuous at $f(a)$ is continuous
  at $a$.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:monotone-continuity-criterion]
{Continuity Criterion for Monotone Functions}}{%
  Continuity Criterion for Monotone Functions}
nodes.question:
When is an increasing function continuous at an interior
  point $c$?
nodes.statement_display:
Exactly when the left and right limits both equal $f(c)$,
  equivalently when $\sup\{f(x): x < c\} = f(c) =
  \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Exactly when the left and right limits both equal $f(c)$,
  equivalently when $\sup\{f(x): x < c\} = f(c) =
  \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$.
nodes.hint:
No gap between the left sup and the right inf at $c$ —
  the jump is zero.
nodes.formal:
$f$ continuous at $c$
$\;\iff\;$
$f(c^-) = f(c) = f(c^+)$
$\;\iff\;$
$\sup\{f(x): x < c\} = f(c) = \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$f$ continuous at $c$
$\;\iff\;$
$f(c^-) = f(c) = f(c^+)$
$\;\iff\;$
$\sup\{f(x): x < c\} = f(c) = \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$.
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. $(\Rightarrow)$: continuity gives two-sided limit, so both one-sided
limits exist and equal $f(c)$; in particular they are equal. 2. $(\Leftarrow)$:
one-sided limits exist by \#116; ordering $f(c^{-}) \leq f(c) \leq f(c^{+})$ plus
$f(c^{-}) = f(c^{+})$ forces all three equal; hence two-sided limit $= f(c)$.

statement.plain:
Exactly when the left and right limits both equal $f(c)$,
  equivalently when $\sup\{f(x): x < c\} = f(c) =
  \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$.

formal.plain:
$f$ continuous at $c$
$\;\iff\;$
$f(c^-) = f(c) = f(c^+)$
$\;\iff\;$
$\sup\{f(x): x < c\} = f(c) = \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$.

flash_q.plain:
When is an increasing function continuous at an interior
  point $c$?

flash_a.plain:
Exactly when the left and right limits both equal $f(c)$,
  equivalently when $\sup\{f(x): x < c\} = f(c) =
  \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:monotone-continuous-on-dense-set]
{Monotone Function Is Continuous on a Dense Set}}{%
  Monotone Function Is Continuous on a Dense Set}
nodes.question: Where is a monotone function continuous?
nodes.statement_display:
At a set dense in its domain. The discontinuity set is
  countable, hence nowhere dense, so its complement is dense.
nodes.statement_tex:
At a set dense in its domain. The discontinuity set is
  countable, hence nowhere dense, so its complement is dense.
nodes.hint:
Countable $\Rightarrow$ nowhere dense $\Rightarrow$
  complement is dense.
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. $D$ countable by Discontinuities of Monotone Function Are Countable.
2. Any open subinterval of $I$ is uncountable, so not contained in $D$. 3. Hence
every open subinterval meets $I \setminus D$, so $I \setminus D$ is dense in $I$.

statement.plain:
At a set dense in its domain. The discontinuity set is
  countable, hence nowhere dense, so its complement is dense.

flash_q.plain: Where is a monotone function continuous?

flash_a.plain:
At a set dense in its domain. The discontinuity set is
  countable, hence nowhere dense, so its complement is dense.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:monotone-discontinuities-countable]
{Discontinuities of a Monotone Function Are Countable}}{%
  Discontinuities of a Monotone Function Are Countable}
nodes.question: How many discontinuities can a monotone function have?
nodes.statement_display:
At most countably many. The jump intervals at distinct
  discontinuities are disjoint; each contains a rational; density
  of $\mathbb{Q}$ gives the injection into $\mathbb{Q}$.
nodes.statement_tex:
At most countably many. The jump intervals at distinct
  discontinuities are disjoint; each contains a rational; density
  of $\mathbb{Q}$ gives the injection into $\mathbb{Q}$.
nodes.hint:
Disjoint intervals $\hookrightarrow$ distinct rationals
  $\hookrightarrow$ countable.
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. WLOG $f$ increasing. 2. Each $c \in D$ gives non-empty
$J_c(f) = (f(c^-), f(c^+))$; pick $r(c) \in J_c(f) \cap \mathbb{Q}$ by density.
3. Jump intervals are pairwise disjoint (Jump Intervals corollary), so $r$ is
injective. 4. Injection $D \hookrightarrow \mathbb{Q}$, $\mathbb{Q}$ countable,
so $D$ at most countable.

statement.plain:
At most countably many. The jump intervals at distinct
  discontinuities are disjoint; each contains a rational; density
  of $\mathbb{Q}$ gives the injection into $\mathbb{Q}$.

flash_q.plain: How many discontinuities can a monotone function have?

flash_a.plain:
At most countably many. The jump intervals at distinct
  discontinuities are disjoint; each contains a rational; density
  of $\mathbb{Q}$ gives the injection into $\mathbb{Q}$.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:monotone-discontinuities-first-kind]
{Discontinuities of a Monotone Function Are of First Kind}}{%
  Discontinuities of a Monotone Function Are of First Kind}
nodes.question: What kind of discontinuities can a monotone function have?
nodes.statement_display:
Only discontinuities of first kind — both one-sided limits
  exist at every discontinuity. Oscillation is impossible for a
  monotone function.
nodes.statement_tex:
Only discontinuities of first kind — both one-sided limits
  exist at every discontinuity. Oscillation is impossible for a
  monotone function.
nodes.hint:
Monotonicity forces all nearby values to lie on the same
  side, so one-sided limits must converge. No oscillation is possible.

statement.plain:
Only discontinuities of first kind — both one-sided limits
  exist at every discontinuity. Oscillation is impossible for a
  monotone function.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:monotone-one-sided-limits]
{One-Sided Limits of Monotone Functions}}{%
  One-Sided Limits of Monotone Functions}
nodes.question:
What are the one-sided limits of an increasing function at
  an interior point $c$?
nodes.statement_display:
$\lim_{x \to c^-} f = \sup\{f(x): x < c\}$ and
  $\lim_{x \to c^+} f = \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$; both exist.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\lim_{x \to c^-} f = \sup\{f(x): x < c\}$ and
  $\lim_{x \to c^+} f = \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$; both exist.
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. Set $s = \sup S$, $j = \inf J$; both finite by boundedness from interior
point. 2. For $\lim_{x \to c^{-}}$: $s - \varepsilon$ not an upper bound gives $x_0 
c$; take $\delta = c - x_0$; monotonicity pins $f(x) \in (s-\varepsilon, s]$. 3.
Right-hand limit is the mirror via $\inf J$. 4. Ordering follows from monotonicity
applied to all $x < c$ (resp.\ $x > c$).

statement.plain:
$\lim_{x \to c^-} f = \sup\{f(x): x < c\}$ and
  $\lim_{x \to c^+} f = \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$; both exist.

flash_q.plain:
What are the one-sided limits of an increasing function at
  an interior point $c$?

flash_a.plain:
$\lim_{x \to c^-} f = \sup\{f(x): x < c\}$ and
  $\lim_{x \to c^+} f = \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$; both exist.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:non-uniform-continuity-criteria]
{Non-Uniform Continuity Criteria}}{%
  Non-Uniform Continuity Criteria}
nodes.question: State the sequential criterion for failure of uniform continuity.
nodes.statement_display:
$f$ fails to be uniformly continuous on $A$ iff there exist
  $\varepsilon_0 > 0$ and sequences $(x_n),(u_n) \subseteq A$ with
  $x_n - u_n \to 0$ but $|f(x_n)-f(u_n)| \geq \varepsilon_0$ for all $n$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$f$ fails to be uniformly continuous on $A$ iff there exist
  $\varepsilon_0 > 0$ and sequences $(x_n),(u_n) \subseteq A$ with
  $x_n - u_n \to 0$ but $|f(x_n)-f(u_n)| \geq \varepsilon_0$ for all $n$.
nodes.hint:
Inputs squeeze together; outputs stay apart. This is the
  negation of uniform continuity with $\delta = 1/n$.
nodes.negation_display:
$f(x)=x^2$ on $\mathbb{R}$: take $x_n = n+1/n$,
  $u_n = n$; then $x_n - u_n = 1/n \to 0$ but
  $|f(x_n)-f(u_n)| = 2 + 1/n^2 \not\to 0$.
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  $(\Rightarrow)$: Negate UC; set $\delta=1/n$ to get $(x_n),(u_n)$ with
  $|x_n-u_n|<1/n$ and $|f(x_n)-f(u_n)|\geq\varepsilon_0$. Squeeze gives
  $x_n-u_n\to 0$. \\
  $(\Leftarrow)$: Suppose UC holds; get $\delta>0$. Since $x_n-u_n\to 0$,
  pick $N$ with $|x_N-u_N|<\delta$, giving $|f(x_N)-f(u_N)|<\varepsilon_0$.
  Contradicts $|f(x_n)-f(u_n)|\geq\varepsilon_0$ for all $n$.

statement.plain:
$f$ fails to be uniformly continuous on $A$ iff there exist
  $\varepsilon_0 > 0$ and sequences $(x_n),(u_n) \subseteq A$ with
  $x_n - u_n \to 0$ but $|f(x_n)-f(u_n)| \geq \varepsilon_0$ for all $n$.

negation.plain:
$f(x)=x^2$ on $\mathbb{R}$: take $x_n = n+1/n$,
  $u_n = n$; then $x_n - u_n = 1/n \to 0$ but
  $|f(x_n)-f(u_n)| = 2 + 1/n^2 \not\to 0$.

flash_a.plain:

  \hyperref[prf:rational-functions-continuous]
{Rational Functions Are Continuous on Their Domain}}{%
  Rational Functions Are Continuous on Their Domain}
nodes.question: State the continuity theorem for rational functions.
nodes.statement_display:
If $P$ and $Q$ are real polynomials and $D = \{x \in \mathbb{R} : Q(x) \neq 0\}$,
  then $R(x) = P(x)/Q(x)$ is continuous on $D$.
nodes.statement_tex:
If $P$ and $Q$ are real polynomials and $D = \{x \in \mathbb{R} : Q(x) \neq 0\}$,
  then $R(x) = P(x)/Q(x)$ is continuous on $D$.
nodes.hint:
Polynomials are continuous everywhere; quotient rule for continuous
  functions applies whenever the denominator is nonzero.
nodes.formal:
$\forall$ real polynomials $P$, $Q$,
$\forall x_0 \in D = \{x \in \mathbb{R} : Q(x) \neq 0\}$:
$R = P/Q$ is continuous at $x_0$.

$\forall$ real polynomials $P$, $Q$,
$\forall x_0 \in D = \{x \in \mathbb{R} : Q(x) \neq 0\}$:
$R = P/Q$ is continuous at $x_0$.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:sequential-uniform-continuity]
{Sequential Characterization of Uniform Continuity}}{%
  Sequential Characterization of Uniform Continuity}
nodes.question: State the sequential characterisation of uniform continuity.
nodes.statement_display:
$f$ is uniformly continuous iff whenever $(x_n) \sim (y_n)$
  in $X$ (i.e., $x_n - y_n \to 0$), we have $f(x_n) - f(y_n) \to 0$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$f$ is uniformly continuous iff whenever $(x_n) \sim (y_n)$
  in $X$ (i.e., $x_n - y_n \to 0$), we have $f(x_n) - f(y_n) \to 0$.
nodes.hint:
Equivalent inputs produce equivalent outputs. Compare with
  pointwise continuity where only one sequence moves toward a fixed
  point.
nodes.negation_display:
$f(x)=1/x$ on $(0,1)$: take $x_n = 1/n$, $y_n = 1/2n$;
  then $x_n - y_n \to 0$ but $f(x_n)-f(y_n) = n - 2n = -n \not\to 0$.
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  $(\Rightarrow)$: UC gives $\delta$ for $\varepsilon$. Since $|x_n-y_n|\to 0$,
  pick $N$ with $|x_n-y_n|<\delta$ for $n>N$. Then $|f(x_n)-f(y_n)|<\varepsilon$
  for all $n>N$. As $\varepsilon$ was arbitrary, $|f(x_n)-f(y_n)|\to 0$. \\
  $(\Leftarrow)$: If $f$ not UC, Non-Uniform Continuity Criteria gives
  $(x_n),(y_n)$ with $|x_n-y_n|\to 0$ but $|f(x_n)-f(y_n)|\geq\varepsilon_0$
  for all $n$ — contradicting the hypothesis.

statement.plain:
$f$ is uniformly continuous iff whenever $(x_n) \sim (y_n)$
  in $X$ (i.e., $x_n - y_n \to 0$), we have $f(x_n) - f(y_n) \to 0$.

negation.plain:
$f(x)=1/x$ on $(0,1)$: take $x_n = 1/n$, $y_n = 1/2n$;
  then $x_n - y_n \to 0$ but $f(x_n)-f(y_n) = n - 2n = -n \not\to 0$.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:sign-preservation]
{Sign Preservation}}{%
  Sign Preservation}
nodes.question: State Sign Preservation for continuous functions.
nodes.statement_display:
If $f$ is continuous at $x_0$ and $f(x_0) \neq 0$, then $f$ preserves
  the sign of $f(x_0)$ on some neighbourhood of $x_0$.
nodes.statement_tex:
If $f$ is continuous at $x_0$ and $f(x_0) \neq 0$, then $f$ preserves
  the sign of $f(x_0)$ on some neighbourhood of $x_0$.
nodes.hint:
Apply continuity with $\varepsilon = |f(x_0)|/2$; the resulting
  $\delta$-neighbourhood keeps $f$ bounded away from zero on the same side.
nodes.formal:
$f$ continuous at $x_0$, $f(x_0) > 0$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$(\exists\,\delta > 0)(\forall x \in E)\;|x - x_0| < \delta \Rightarrow f(x) > 0$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$f$ continuous at $x_0$, $f(x_0) > 0$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$(\exists\,\delta > 0)(\forall x \in E)\;|x - x_0| < \delta \Rightarrow f(x) > 0$.
nodes.negation_display:
Fails without continuity: $f(x) = \mathrm{sgn}(x)$ is positive at
  $x_0 = 1$ but the sign flips arbitrarily close to $x_0 = 0$.
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. Set $\varepsilon_0 = |f(x_0)| > 0$; continuity gives $\delta$ with
     $|x-x_0|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(x_0)| < |f(x_0)|$. \\
  2. Unpack: $f(x_0) - |f(x_0)| < f(x) < f(x_0) + |f(x_0)|$. \\
  3. If $f(x_0)>0$: lower bound gives $f(x) > 0$.
     If $f(x_0)<0$: upper bound gives $f(x) < 0$.

statement.plain:
If $f$ is continuous at $x_0$ and $f(x_0) \neq 0$, then $f$ preserves
  the sign of $f(x_0)$ on some neighbourhood of $x_0$.

formal.plain:
$f$ continuous at $x_0$, $f(x_0) > 0$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$(\exists\,\delta > 0)(\forall x \in E)\;|x - x_0| < \delta \Rightarrow f(x) > 0$.

negation.plain:
Fails without continuity: $f(x) = \mathrm{sgn}(x)$ is positive at
  $x_0 = 1$ but the sign flips arbitrarily close to $x_0 = 0$.

flash_a.plain:

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:uc-bijection-compact-inverse-uc]
{Uniformly Continuous Bijection on a Compact Domain Has Uniformly Continuous Inverse}}{%
  Uniformly Continuous Bijection on a Compact Domain Has Uniformly Continuous Inverse}
nodes.question:
If $f : [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is a continuous bijection,
  is $f^{-1}$ uniformly continuous?
nodes.statement_display:
Yes. $f^{-1}$ is continuous by the Continuous Inverse
  Theorem, and UC by Heine–Cantor since $f([a,b])$ is a compact
  interval.
nodes.statement_tex:
Yes. $f^{-1}$ is continuous by the Continuous Inverse
  Theorem, and UC by Heine–Cantor since $f([a,b])$ is a compact
  interval.
nodes.hint:
Two-step: CIT gives continuity of $f^{-1}$, then
  Heine–Cantor promotes it to UC on the compact image.
nodes.negation_display:
On non-compact domains neither step is guaranteed:
  $f^{-1}$ need not be continuous, and even if continuous need
  not be UC.

statement.plain:
Yes. $f^{-1}$ is continuous by the Continuous Inverse
  Theorem, and UC by Heine–Cantor since $f([a,b])$ is a compact
  interval.

negation.plain:
On non-compact domains neither step is guaranteed:
  $f^{-1}$ need not be continuous, and even if continuous need
  not be UC.

flash_a.plain:

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:uc-space-is-convex]
{The Space of Uniformly Continuous Functions Is Convex}}{%
  The Space of Uniformly Continuous Functions Is Convex}
nodes.question: Is the set of uniformly continuous functions on $A$ convex?
nodes.statement_display:
Yes. If $f_1, f_2$ are UC and $t \in [0,1]$, then
  $(1-t)f_1 + tf_2$ is UC. Proof: one-line consequence of the
  algebra of uniform continuity.
nodes.statement_tex:
Yes. If $f_1, f_2$ are UC and $t \in [0,1]$, then
  $(1-t)f_1 + tf_2$ is UC. Proof: one-line consequence of the
  algebra of uniform continuity.
nodes.hint:
Convex combination is a linear combination with
  non-negative coefficients summing to 1. The algebra theorem
  gives UC for any linear combination, so convexity is immediate.
nodes.negation_display:
The set of Lipschitz functions with a \emph{fixed}
  constant $K$ is also convex (the constant of the combination
  is at most $K$), but the set of all Lipschitz functions
  (varying constants) is a vector space, not just convex.

statement.plain:

  $(1-t)f_1 + tf_2$ is UC. Proof: one-line consequence of the

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:bolzano-intermediate-value]
{Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}}{Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}
nodes.question: State Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem.
nodes.statement_display: If \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on an interval \(I\), and if \(a,b\in I\) with \(f(a)<k<f(b)\), then there exists a point \(c\) between \(a\) and \(b\) such that \(f(c)=k\).
nodes.statement_tex: If \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on an interval \(I\), and if \(a,b\in I\) with \(f(a)<k<f(b)\), then there exists a point \(c\) between \(a\) and \(b\) such that \(f(c)=k\).
nodes.hint: Subtract \(k\) and reduce to the Location of Roots Theorem.
nodes.formal:
Let \(I\subseteq\mathbb{R}\) be an interval and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\ \wedge\
a\in I
\ \wedge\
b\in I
\ \wedge\
f(a)<k<f(b)
\]
implies
\[
\exists c\in I
\text{ such that }
\min\{a,b\}<c<\max\{a,b\}
\text{ and }
f(c)=k.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(I\subseteq\mathbb{R}\) be an interval and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\ \wedge\
a\in I
\ \wedge\
b\in I
\ \wedge\
f(a)<k<f(b)
\]
implies
\[
\exists c\in I
\text{ such that }
\min\{a,b\}<c<\max\{a,b\}
\text{ and }
f(c)=k.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
Set $g = f - k$. Continuity of $g$ is free from algebra of
continuous functions. $g(a) < 0 < g(b)$ by hypothesis. Location of
Roots gives $c$ between $a$ and $b$ with $g(c) = 0$, i.e., $f(c) = k$.
Second case: apply to $-f$.
nodes.uses_text: Algebra of continuous functions, Location of Roots Theorem

statement.plain: If \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on an interval \(I\), and if \(a,b\in I\) with \(f(a)<k<f(b)\), then there exists a point \(c\) between \(a\) and \(b\) such that \(f(c)=k\).

formal.plain:
Let \(I\subseteq\mathbb{R}\) be an interval and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\ \wedge\
a\in I
\ \wedge\
b\in I
\ \wedge\
f(a)<k<f(b)
\]
implies
\[
\exists c\in I
\text{ such that }
\min\{a,b\}<c<\max\{a,b\}
\text{ and }
f(c)=k.
\]

flash_a.plain: If \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on an interval \(I\), and if \(a,b\in I\) with \(f(a)<k<f(b)\), then there exists a point \(c\) between \(a\) and \(b\) such that \(f(c)=k\).

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:boundedness-theorem]
{Boundedness Theorem}}{Boundedness Theorem}
nodes.question: What does continuity on a closed bounded interval guarantee first?
nodes.statement_display: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), then \(f\) is bounded on \([a,b]\).
nodes.statement_tex: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), then \(f\) is bounded on \([a,b]\).
nodes.hint: This is the first compactness consequence for continuous functions on closed intervals.
nodes.formal:
For all \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a\le b\), for every function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\),
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\exists M>0\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ |f(x)|\le M.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
For all \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a\le b\), for every function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\),
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\exists M>0\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ |f(x)|\le M.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. Assume $f$ unbounded: choose $x_n \in I$ with $|f(x_n)| > n$. \\
  2. $(x_n) \subseteq [a,b]$ bounded $\xrightarrow{\text{B-W}}$
     subsequence $x_{n_k} \to x^* \in I$ (closed). \\
  3. Continuity: $f(x_{n_k}) \to f(x^*)$, so image subsequence bounded;
     but $|f(x_{n_k})| > n_k \to \infty$. Contradiction.

statement.plain: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), then \(f\) is bounded on \([a,b]\).

formal.plain:
For all \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a\le b\), for every function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\),
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\exists M>0\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ |f(x)|\le M.
\]

flash_q.plain: What does continuity on a closed bounded interval guarantee first?

flash_a.plain: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), then \(f\) is bounded on \([a,b]\).

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:continuity-algebra-at-a-point]
{Algebra of Continuity at a Point}}{Algebra of Continuity at a Point}
nodes.question: Which algebraic operations preserve continuity at a point?
nodes.statement_display: If \(f\) and \(g\) are continuous at \(c\), then \(f+g\), \(f-g\), \(fg\), and \(bf\) are continuous at \(c\). If \(h\) is continuous at \(c\) and never vanishes on \(A\), then \(f/h\) is continuous at \(c\).
nodes.statement_tex: If \(f\) and \(g\) are continuous at \(c\), then \(f+g\), \(f-g\), \(fg\), and \(bf\) are continuous at \(c\). If \(h\) is continuous at \(c\) and never vanishes on \(A\), then \(f/h\) is continuous at \(c\).
nodes.hint: Reduce continuity to limits at the point, then apply the algebra of limits of functions.
nodes.formal:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}\), let \(b \in \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in A\).
  1. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ g \text{ is continuous at } c\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f+g,\ f-g,\ fg,\ bf \text{ are continuous at } c\bigr). \]
  2. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ h \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ \forall x\in A,\ h(x)\neq 0\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f/h \text{ is continuous at } c\bigr). \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}\), let \(b \in \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in A\).
  1. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ g \text{ is continuous at } c\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f+g,\ f-g,\ fg,\ bf \text{ are continuous at } c\bigr). \]
  2. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ h \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ \forall x\in A,\ h(x)\neq 0\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f/h \text{ is continuous at } c\bigr). \]
nodes.proof_sketch: 1. Rewrite continuity at c as the limit identities lim_{x->c} f(x)=f(c) and lim_{x->c} g(x)=g(c). 2. Apply the sum, difference, product, and scalar-multiple rules for limits to obtain continuity of f+g, f-g, fg, and bf at c. 3. For the quotient part, use continuity of h at c together with h(x) != 0 on A to get h(c) != 0, then apply the quotient rule for limits.
nodes.uses_text: def:continuous-at-a-point, thm:limit-function-sum, thm:limit-function-difference, thm:limit-function-product, thm:limit-function-quotient, thm:limit-function-scalar

formal.plain:

  1. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ g \text{ is continuous at } c\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f+g,\ f-g,\ fg,\ bf \text{ are continuous at } c\bigr). \]
  2. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ h \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ \forall x\in A,\ h(x)\neq 0\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f/h \text{ is continuous at } c\bigr). \]

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:continuity-algebra-on-a-set]
{Algebra of Continuity on a Set}}{Algebra of Continuity on a Set}
nodes.question: Which algebraic operations preserve continuity on a set?
nodes.statement_display: If \(f\) and \(g\) are continuous on \(A\), then \(f+g\), \(f-g\), \(fg\), and \(bf\) are continuous on \(A\). If \(h\) is continuous on \(A\) and never vanishes on \(A\), then \(f/h\) is continuous on \(A\).
nodes.statement_tex: If \(f\) and \(g\) are continuous on \(A\), then \(f+g\), \(f-g\), \(fg\), and \(bf\) are continuous on \(A\). If \(h\) is continuous on \(A\) and never vanishes on \(A\), then \(f/h\) is continuous on \(A\).
nodes.hint: This is the pointwise theorem applied at an arbitrary \(c\in A\).
nodes.formal:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(b \in \mathbb{R}\).
  1. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous on } A \ \wedge\ g \text{ is continuous on } A\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f+g,\ f-g,\ fg,\ bf \text{ are continuous on } A\bigr). \]
  2. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous on } A \ \wedge\ h \text{ is continuous on } A \ \wedge\ \forall x\in A,\ h(x)\neq 0\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f/h \text{ is continuous on } A\bigr). \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(b \in \mathbb{R}\).
  1. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous on } A \ \wedge\ g \text{ is continuous on } A\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f+g,\ f-g,\ fg,\ bf \text{ are continuous on } A\bigr). \]
  2. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous on } A \ \wedge\ h \text{ is continuous on } A \ \wedge\ \forall x\in A,\ h(x)\neq 0\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f/h \text{ is continuous on } A\bigr). \]
nodes.proof_sketch: 1. Fix an arbitrary c in A. 2. Since f and g are continuous on A, they are continuous at c. Apply the pointwise algebra-of-continuity theorem to obtain continuity of f+g, f-g, fg, and bf at c. 3. For the quotient part, use that h is continuous on A and nonzero on A, hence continuous at c and nonzero there. Apply the pointwise quotient result. 4. Since c was arbitrary, the derived functions are continuous on A.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:discontinuity-on-a-set]
{Discontinuity on a Set Is Pointwise Failure Somewhere}}{Discontinuity on a Set Is Pointwise Failure Somewhere}
nodes.question: How is discontinuity on a set related to pointwise discontinuity?
nodes.statement_display: A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is discontinuous on \(E\) if and only if there exists a point \(x_0\in E\) where it is discontinuous. By the equivalence lemma, the bad point may be witnessed in epsilon-delta, sequential, or neighborhood form.
nodes.statement_tex: A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is discontinuous on \(E\) if and only if there exists a point \(x_0\in E\) where it is discontinuous. By the equivalence lemma, the bad point may be witnessed in epsilon-delta, sequential, or neighborhood form.
nodes.hint: Set-level discontinuity is just one outer existential quantifier added to the pointwise notion.
nodes.formal:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) and let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\). Then the following are equivalent:
  1. \[ f \text{ is discontinuous on } E. \]
  2. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E: \bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr). \]
  3. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists (x_n)\subseteq E: \quad x_n\to x_0 \quad\text{and}\quad f(x_n)\not\to f(x_0). \]
  4. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists V \text{ a neighborhood of } f(x_0)\; \forall U \text{ a neighborhood of } x_0\; \exists x\in U\cap E: \quad f(x)\notin V. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) and let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\). Then the following are equivalent:
  1. \[ f \text{ is discontinuous on } E. \]
  2. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E: \bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr). \]
  3. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists (x_n)\subseteq E: \quad x_n\to x_0 \quad\text{and}\quad f(x_n)\not\to f(x_0). \]
  4. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists V \text{ a neighborhood of } f(x_0)\; \forall U \text{ a neighborhood of } x_0\; \exists x\in U\cap E: \quad f(x)\notin V. \]

statement.plain: A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is discontinuous on \(E\) if and only if there exists a point \(x_0\in E\) where it is discontinuous. By the equivalence lemma, the bad point may be witnessed in epsilon-delta, sequential, or neighborhood form.

formal.plain:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) and let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\). Then the following are equivalent:
  1. \[ f \text{ is discontinuous on } E. \]
  2. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E: \bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr). \]
  3. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists (x_n)\subseteq E: \quad x_n\to x_0 \quad\text{and}\quad f(x_n)\not\to f(x_0). \]
  4. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists V \text{ a neighborhood of } f(x_0)\; \forall U \text{ a neighborhood of } x_0\; \exists x\in U\cap E: \quad f(x)\notin V. \]

flash_a.plain: A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is discontinuous on \(E\) if and only if there exists a point \(x_0\in E\) where it is discontinuous. By the equivalence lemma, the bad point may be witnessed in epsilon-delta, sequential, or neighborhood form.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:equivalent-discontinuity-at-a-point]
{Equivalent Formulations of Discontinuity at a Point}}{Equivalent Formulations of Discontinuity at a Point}
nodes.question: What are the equivalent formulations of discontinuity at a point?
nodes.statement_display: For \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) and \(x_0\in E\), the epsilon-delta, sequential, and neighborhood characterizations of discontinuity at \(x_0\) are equivalent.
nodes.statement_tex: For \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) and \(x_0\in E\), the epsilon-delta, sequential, and neighborhood characterizations of discontinuity at \(x_0\) are equivalent.
nodes.hint: This is the failure-version of the standard equivalence theorem for continuity.
nodes.formal:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0 \in E\). Then the following are equivalent:
  1. \[ \exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E: \bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr). \]
  2. \[ \exists (x_n)\subseteq E: \quad x_n\to x_0 \quad\text{and}\quad f(x_n)\not\to f(x_0). \]
  3. \[ \exists V \text{ a neighborhood of } f(x_0)\; \forall U \text{ a neighborhood of } x_0\; \exists x\in U\cap E: \quad f(x)\notin V. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0 \in E\). Then the following are equivalent:
  1. \[ \exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E: \bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr). \]
  2. \[ \exists (x_n)\subseteq E: \quad x_n\to x_0 \quad\text{and}\quad f(x_n)\not\to f(x_0). \]
  3. \[ \exists V \text{ a neighborhood of } f(x_0)\; \forall U \text{ a neighborhood of } x_0\; \exists x\in U\cap E: \quad f(x)\notin V. \]
nodes.proof_sketch: 1. Prove epsilon-delta discontinuity implies sequential discontinuity by choosing x_n with delta=1/n. 2. Prove sequential discontinuity implies epsilon-delta discontinuity by using the negation of convergence of f(x_n). 3. Rewrite the epsilon-delta condition in neighborhood language using open intervals around x_0 and f(x_0).

statement.plain: For \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) and \(x_0\in E\), the epsilon-delta, sequential, and neighborhood characterizations of discontinuity at \(x_0\) are equivalent.

formal.plain:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0 \in E\). Then the following are equivalent:
  1. \[ \exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E: \bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr). \]
  2. \[ \exists (x_n)\subseteq E: \quad x_n\to x_0 \quad\text{and}\quad f(x_n)\not\to f(x_0). \]
  3. \[ \exists V \text{ a neighborhood of } f(x_0)\; \forall U \text{ a neighborhood of } x_0\; \exists x\in U\cap E: \quad f(x)\notin V. \]

flash_a.plain: For \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) and \(x_0\in E\), the epsilon-delta, sequential, and neighborhood characterizations of discontinuity at \(x_0\) are equivalent.

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:image-of-closed-bounded-interval]
{Image of a Closed Bounded Interval}}{Image of a Closed Bounded Interval}
nodes.question: What is the image of a closed bounded interval under a continuous function?
nodes.statement_display: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous, then \(f([a,b])\) is a closed bounded interval.
nodes.statement_tex: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous, then \(f([a,b])\) is a closed bounded interval.
nodes.hint: Boundedness gives bounded, extrema give endpoints, and IVT removes gaps.
nodes.formal:
Let \(I=[a,b]\subseteq\mathbb{R}\), and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
f(I) \text{ is a closed bounded interval.}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(I=[a,b]\subseteq\mathbb{R}\), and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
f(I) \text{ is a closed bounded interval.}
\]

statement.plain: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous, then \(f([a,b])\) is a closed bounded interval.

formal.plain:
Let \(I=[a,b]\subseteq\mathbb{R}\), and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
f(I) \text{ is a closed bounded interval.}
\]

flash_q.plain: What is the image of a closed bounded interval under a continuous function?

flash_a.plain: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous, then \(f([a,b])\) is a closed bounded interval.

{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}}{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:ivt-between-inf-and-sup]
{Every Value Between \(\inf f(I)\) and \(\sup f(I)\) Is Attained}}{Every Value Between inf f(I) and sup f(I) Is Attained}
nodes.question: What does IVT say on a closed bounded interval after extrema are known to exist?
nodes.statement_display: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous and \(k\) satisfies \(\inf f([a,b])\le k\le \sup f([a,b])\), then there exists \(c\in[a,b]\) such that \(f(c)=k\).
nodes.statement_tex: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous and \(k\) satisfies \(\inf f([a,b])\le k\le \sup f([a,b])\), then there exists \(c\in[a,b]\) such that \(f(c)=k\).
nodes.hint: Use the Maximum–Minimum Theorem to identify points attaining the infimum and supremum, then apply IVT.
nodes.formal:
Let \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a\le b\), and let \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\ \wedge\
\inf f([a,b])\le k\le \sup f([a,b])
\]
implies
\[
\exists c\in[a,b]\text{ such that }f(c)=k.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a\le b\), and let \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\ \wedge\
\inf f([a,b])\le k\le \sup f([a,b])
\]
implies
\[
\exists c\in[a,b]\text{ such that }f(c)=k.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
EVT gives $x_m, x_M$ with $f(x_m)=m$, $f(x_M)=M$. Set
$g = f-k$; continuous on $J = [\min(x_m,x_M), \max(x_m,x_M)]$.
$g(x_m) = m-k \leq 0$, $g(x_M) = M-k \geq 0$. Three cases: $k=m$
gives $c=x_m$; $k=M$ gives $c=x_M$; $m < k < M$ gives strict
inequalities so Location of Roots applies on $J$.
nodes.uses_text:
Extreme Value Theorem, algebra of continuous functions,
Location of Roots Theorem

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:location-of-roots]
{Location of Roots Theorem}}{Location of Roots Theorem}
nodes.question: What does a sign change force for a continuous function on a closed interval?
nodes.statement_display: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous and \(f(a)\) and \(f(b)\) have opposite signs, then there exists \(c\in(a,b)\) such that \(f(c)=0\).
nodes.statement_tex: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous and \(f(a)\) and \(f(b)\) have opposite signs, then there exists \(c\in(a,b)\) such that \(f(c)=0\).
nodes.hint: A continuous path from negative to positive cannot jump over zero.
nodes.formal:
Let \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a<b\), and let \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\ \wedge\
\bigl(f(a)<0<f(b)\ \vee\ f(a)>0>f(b)\bigr)
\]
implies
\[
\exists c\in(a,b)\text{ such that } f(c)=0.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a<b\), and let \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\ \wedge\
\bigl(f(a)<0<f(b)\ \vee\ f(a)>0>f(b)\bigr)
\]
implies
\[
\exists c\in(a,b)\text{ such that } f(c)=0.
\]

statement.plain: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous and \(f(a)\) and \(f(b)\) have opposite signs, then there exists \(c\in(a,b)\) such that \(f(c)=0\).

formal.plain:
Let \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a<b\), and let \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\ \wedge\
\bigl(f(a)<0<f(b)\ \vee\ f(a)>0>f(b)\bigr)
\]
implies
\[
\exists c\in(a,b)\text{ such that } f(c)=0.
\]

flash_q.plain: What does a sign change force for a continuous function on a closed interval?

flash_a.plain: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous and \(f(a)\) and \(f(b)\) have opposite signs, then there exists \(c\in(a,b)\) such that \(f(c)=0\).

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:maximum-minimum-theorem]
{Maximum--Minimum Theorem}}{Maximum-Minimum Theorem}
nodes.question: What does continuity on a closed bounded interval guarantee about extrema?
nodes.statement_display: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), then \(f\) has an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on \([a,b]\).
nodes.statement_tex: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), then \(f\) has an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on \([a,b]\).
nodes.hint: Continuity on a compact interval forces the extreme values not only to exist as bounds but to be attained.
nodes.formal:
For all \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a\le b\), for every function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\),
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Bigl(
\exists x^{*}\in[a,b]\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ f(x^{*})\ge f(x)
\Bigr)
\]
and
\[
\Bigl(
\exists x_{*}\in[a,b]\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ f(x_{*})\le f(x)
\Bigr).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
For all \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a\le b\), for every function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\),
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Bigl(
\exists x^{*}\in[a,b]\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ f(x^{*})\ge f(x)
\Bigr)
\]
and
\[
\Bigl(
\exists x_{*}\in[a,b]\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ f(x_{*})\le f(x)
\Bigr).
\]
nodes.proof_sketch: 1. Use boundedness to define sup f(I) and inf f(I). 2. Choose sequences whose function values approach these extrema. 3. Extract convergent subsequences in [a,b] and apply continuity to show the extreme values are attained.

statement.plain: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), then \(f\) has an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on \([a,b]\).

formal.plain:
For all \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a\le b\), for every function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\),
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Bigl(
\exists x^{*}\in[a,b]\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ f(x^{*})\ge f(x)
\Bigr)
\]
and
\[
\Bigl(
\exists x_{*}\in[a,b]\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ f(x_{*})\le f(x)
\Bigr).
\]

flash_q.plain: What does continuity on a closed bounded interval guarantee about extrema?

flash_a.plain: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), then \(f\) has an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on \([a,b]\).

nodes.name:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:preservation-of-intervals]
{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}}{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}
nodes.question: What does continuity do to intervals?
nodes.statement_display: A continuous image of an interval is an interval.
nodes.statement_tex: A continuous image of an interval is an interval.
nodes.hint: This is the conceptual endpoint of IVT: continuous functions do not create gaps in images of intervals.
nodes.formal:
Let \(I\subseteq\mathbb{R}\) be an interval and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
f(I) \text{ is an interval.}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(I\subseteq\mathbb{R}\) be an interval and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
f(I) \text{ is an interval.}
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
Take $y_1, y_2 \in f(I)$, $y_1 < k < y_2$. Pull back:
$f(x_1)=y_1$, $f(x_2)=y_2$. $J = [\min(x_1,x_2),\max(x_1,x_2)]
\subseteq I$ since $I$ is an interval. IVT on $J$ gives $c$ with
$f(c)=k$. So $k \in f(I)$. Hence $f(I)$ is closed under betweenness,
i.e., an interval.
nodes.uses_text: Characterization of Intervals, Intermediate Value Theorem

statement.plain: A continuous image of an interval is an interval.

formal.plain:
Let \(I\subseteq\mathbb{R}\) be an interval and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
f(I) \text{ is an interval.}
\]

flash_q.plain: What does continuity do to intervals?

flash_a.plain: A continuous image of an interval is an interval.

nodes.name: def:bi-lipschitz
nodes.question: What is a bi-Lipschitz map?
nodes.statement_display:
A function $f$ satisfying $\alpha|x-u| \leq |f(x)-f(u)|
  \leq K|x-u|$ for constants $0 < \alpha \leq K$. Both an upper
  and a lower Lipschitz bound.
nodes.statement_tex:
A function $f$ satisfying $\alpha|x-u| \leq |f(x)-f(u)|
  \leq K|x-u|$ for constants $0 < \alpha \leq K$. Both an upper
  and a lower Lipschitz bound.
nodes.hint:
Upper bound = Lipschitz. Lower bound = co-Lipschitz
  (expanding). Together they bound the metric distortion in both
  directions.
nodes.negation_display:
$f(x) = x^3$ on $[-1,1]$: Lipschitz ($|f'| \leq 3$) but
  not bi-Lipschitz since $f'(0) = 0$ — the lower bound fails
  near $0$.

statement.plain:
A function $f$ satisfying $\alpha|x-u| \leq |f(x)-f(u)|
  \leq K|x-u|$ for constants $0 < \alpha \leq K$. Both an upper
  and a lower Lipschitz bound.

negation.plain:
$f(x) = x^3$ on $[-1,1]$: Lipschitz ($|f'| \leq 3$) but
  not bi-Lipschitz since $f'(0) = 0$ — the lower bound fails
  near $0$.

flash_a.plain:
A function $f$ satisfying $\alpha|x-u| \leq |f(x)-f(u)|
  \leq K|x-u|$ for constants $0 < \alpha \leq K$. Both an upper
  and a lower Lipschitz bound.

nodes.name: def:continuous-at-point
nodes.question:
State the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition of continuity
  of $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ at $c \in A$.
nodes.statement_display:
$\forall\varepsilon>0,\,\exists\delta>0,\,\forall x\in A:\,
  |x-c|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(c)|<\varepsilon$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\forall\varepsilon>0,\,\exists\delta>0,\,\forall x\in A:\,
  |x-c|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(c)|<\varepsilon$.
nodes.hint:
Three differences from the limit definition: (1) no
  $0 <$ — $x=c$ is permitted; (2) target is $f(c)$, not a
  free $L$; (3) $c$ must lie in $A$.
nodes.negation_display:
$f(x) = \sin(1/x)$ with $f(0) := 0$ is discontinuous
  at $0$: $\sin(1/x)$ oscillates between $\pm 1$ in every
  neighbourhood of $0$, so no $\delta$ works for
  $\varepsilon < 1$.

statement.plain:
$\forall\varepsilon>0,\,\exists\delta>0,\,\forall x\in A:\,
  |x-c|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(c)|<\varepsilon$.

negation.plain:
$f(x) = \sin(1/x)$ with $f(0) := 0$ is discontinuous
  at $0$: $\sin(1/x)$ oscillates between $\pm 1$ in every
  neighbourhood of $0$, so no $\delta$ works for
  $\varepsilon < 1$.

State the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition of continuity
  of $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ at $c \in A$.

flash_a.plain:
$\forall\varepsilon>0,\,\exists\delta>0,\,\forall x\in A:\,
  |x-c|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(c)|<\varepsilon$.

nodes.name: def:continuous-at-point-nbhd
nodes.question:
State the neighbourhood form of continuity of $f$
  at $c \in A$.
nodes.statement_display:
For every $V_\varepsilon(f(c))$ there exists
  $V_\delta(c)$ such that $f(A \cap V_\delta(c)) \subseteq
  V_\varepsilon(f(c))$.
nodes.statement_tex:
For every $V_\varepsilon(f(c))$ there exists
  $V_\delta(c)$ such that $f(A \cap V_\delta(c)) \subseteq
  V_\varepsilon(f(c))$.
nodes.hint:
Same structure as the limit neighbourhood form but
  using the full neighbourhood $V_\delta(c)$, not the punctured
  $V_\delta^*(c)$.

statement.plain:
For every $V_\varepsilon(f(c))$ there exists
  $V_\delta(c)$ such that $f(A \cap V_\delta(c)) \subseteq
  V_\varepsilon(f(c))$.

nodes.name: def:continuous-at-point-seq
nodes.question:
State the sequential definition of continuity of $f$
  at $c \in A$.
nodes.statement_display:
For every $(x_n) \subseteq A$ with $x_n \to c$,
  $f(x_n) \to f(c)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
For every $(x_n) \subseteq A$ with $x_n \to c$,
  $f(x_n) \to f(c)$.
nodes.hint:
No $x_n \neq c$ condition — sequences may hit $c$.
  Contrast with the sequential criterion for limits where
  $x_n \neq c$ was required.
nodes.negation_display:
$f(x)=\sin(1/x)$, $f(0):=0$: take
  $y_n = 1/(\pi/2 + 2n\pi) \to 0$ giving $f(y_n)=1\not\to 0
  = f(0)$. Discontinuous at $0$.

statement.plain:
For every $(x_n) \subseteq A$ with $x_n \to c$,
  $f(x_n) \to f(c)$.

negation.plain:
$f(x)=\sin(1/x)$, $f(0):=0$: take
  $y_n = 1/(\pi/2 + 2n\pi) \to 0$ giving $f(y_n)=1\not\to 0
  = f(0)$. Discontinuous at $0$.

State the sequential definition of continuity of $f$

flash_a.plain:
For every $(x_n) \subseteq A$ with $x_n \to c$,
  $f(x_n) \to f(c)$.

nodes.name: def:discontinuity-of-first-kind
nodes.question: What is a discontinuity of first kind?
nodes.statement_display:
A discontinuity at $a$ where both one-sided limits exist, but
  at least one does not equal $f(a)$. Includes both removable and
  jump discontinuities.
nodes.statement_tex:
A discontinuity at $a$ where both one-sided limits exist, but
  at least one does not equal $f(a)$. Includes both removable and
  jump discontinuities.
nodes.hint:
First kind = both one-sided limits exist. Second kind =
  at least one does not.

statement.plain:
A discontinuity at $a$ where both one-sided limits exist, but
  at least one does not equal $f(a)$. Includes both removable and
  jump discontinuities.

nodes.name: def:discontinuity-of-second-kind
nodes.question: What is a discontinuity of second kind?
nodes.statement_display:
A discontinuity where at least one one-sided limit fails to
  exist. All discontinuities not of first kind are of second kind.
nodes.statement_tex:
A discontinuity where at least one one-sided limit fails to
  exist. All discontinuities not of first kind are of second kind.
nodes.hint:
Second kind = at least one one-sided limit missing.
  Infinite blow-up and oscillation are both second kind.

statement.plain:
A discontinuity where at least one one-sided limit fails to
  exist. All discontinuities not of first kind are of second kind.

flash_q.plain: What is a discontinuity of second kind?

flash_a.plain:
A discontinuity where at least one one-sided limit fails to
  exist. All discontinuities not of first kind are of second kind.

nodes.name: def:equivalent-sequences
nodes.question: Define equivalent sequences.
nodes.statement_display:
$(a_n) \sim (b_n)$ iff $\lim_{n \to \infty}(a_n - b_n) = 0$,
  i.e., they eventually become arbitrarily close to each other.
nodes.statement_tex:
$(a_n) \sim (b_n)$ iff $\lim_{n \to \infty}(a_n - b_n) = 0$,
  i.e., they eventually become arbitrarily close to each other.
nodes.hint:

  $(b_n) = (L)$ is constant.

nodes.name: def:infinite-adherent-points
nodes.question: When is $+\infty$ adherent to a set $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$?
nodes.statement_display:
When $X$ is unbounded above, i.e., $\forall M \in \mathbb{R}$,
  $\exists x \in X$ with $x > M$, equivalently $\sup X = +\infty$.
nodes.statement_tex:
When $X$ is unbounded above, i.e., $\forall M \in \mathbb{R}$,
  $\exists x \in X$ with $x > M$, equivalently $\sup X = +\infty$.
nodes.hint:
The infinite analogue of: $x$ is adherent to $X$ iff $X$
  has elements arbitrarily close to $x$.

statement.plain:
When $X$ is unbounded above, i.e., $\forall M \in \mathbb{R}$,
  $\exists x \in X$ with $x > M$, equivalently $\sup X = +\infty$.

nodes.name: def:jump-of-function
nodes.question: Define the jump of an increasing function $f$ at $c$.
nodes.statement_display:
$j_f(c) = \lim_{x \to c^+} f(x) - \lim_{x \to c^-} f(x)$.
  Continuity at $c$ iff $j_f(c) = 0$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$j_f(c) = \lim_{x \to c^+} f(x) - \lim_{x \to c^-} f(x)$.
  Continuity at $c$ iff $j_f(c) = 0$.
nodes.hint:
The jump measures the gap in the graph at $c$. Positive
  jump = jump discontinuity; zero jump = continuous.

statement.plain:
$j_f(c) = \lim_{x \to c^+} f(x) - \lim_{x \to c^-} f(x)$.
  Continuity at $c$ iff $j_f(c) = 0$.

flash_a.plain:

nodes.name: def:limit-at-infinity
nodes.question: Define $\lim_{x \to +\infty} f(x) = L$.
nodes.statement_display:
$\forall\varepsilon > 0,\;\exists M \in \mathbb{R}:\;
  x \in X,\, x > M \Rightarrow |f(x)-L| < \varepsilon$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\forall\varepsilon > 0,\;\exists M \in \mathbb{R}:\;
  x \in X,\, x > M \Rightarrow |f(x)-L| < \varepsilon$.
nodes.hint:
Replace $|x - c| < \delta$ with $x > M$. Same
  $\varepsilon$ condition on the output.
nodes.negation_display:
$f(x) = \sin x$: $\lim_{x \to +\infty} \sin x$ does not
  exist since $\sin x$ oscillates between $-1$ and $1$ for all large
  $x$.

statement.plain:
$\forall\varepsilon > 0,\;\exists M \in \mathbb{R}:\;
  x \in X,\, x > M \Rightarrow |f(x)-L| < \varepsilon$.

nodes.name: def:limsup-liminf-function
nodes.question: Define $\limsup_{x \to x_0} f(x)$.
nodes.statement_display:
$\inf_{\delta > 0} \sup\{f(x) : x \in E,\; 0 < |x-x_0| <
  \delta\}$. The infimum over shrinking neighbourhoods of the
  supremum of $f$ values nearby.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\inf_{\delta > 0} \sup\{f(x) : x \in E,\; 0 < |x-x_0| <
  \delta\}$. The infimum over shrinking neighbourhoods of the
  supremum of $f$ values nearby.
nodes.hint:
Shrink the neighbourhood; the sup of $f$ on it can only
  decrease; the limit of that decreasing function is the $\limsup$.
nodes.negation_display:
$f(x) = \sin(1/x)$ at $x_0 = 0$: $\limsup = 1$,
  $\liminf = -1$. The ordinary limit does not exist.

statement.plain:
$\inf_{\delta > 0} \sup\{f(x) : x \in E,\; 0 < |x-x_0| <
  \delta\}$. The infimum over shrinking neighbourhoods of the
  supremum of $f$ values nearby.

nodes.name: def:lipschitz-condition
nodes.question: Define a Lipschitz function $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_display:
$f$ is Lipschitz if $\exists K > 0$ such that
  $|f(x)-f(u)| \leq K|x-u|$ for all $x,u \in A$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$f$ is Lipschitz if $\exists K > 0$ such that
  $|f(x)-f(u)| \leq K|x-u|$ for all $x,u \in A$.
nodes.hint:
The secant slope interpretation: all chords on the graph
  have slope $\leq K$ in absolute value.
nodes.negation_display:
$\sqrt{x}$ on $[0,1]$: secant slopes through $(0,0)$ are
  $1/\sqrt{x} \to \infty$ as $x \to 0^+$, so no $K$ works.

statement.plain:
$f$ is Lipschitz if $\exists K > 0$ such that
  $|f(x)-f(u)| \leq K|x-u|$ for all $x,u \in A$.

negation.plain:
$\sqrt{x}$ on $[0,1]$: secant slopes through $(0,0)$ are
  $1/\sqrt{x} \to \infty$ as $x \to 0^+$, so no $K$ works.

flash_a.plain:

  $|f(x)-f(u)| \leq K|x-u|$ for all $x,u \in A$.

nodes.name: def:point-of-discontinuity
nodes.question: What is a point of discontinuity?
nodes.statement_display:
A point $a \in E$ where $f$ is not continuous. Equivalently,
  $\exists\varepsilon_0>0$ such that no $\delta>0$ keeps $f$ within
  $\varepsilon_0$ of $f(a)$ on all of $U_\delta(a) \cap E$.
nodes.statement_tex:
A point $a \in E$ where $f$ is not continuous. Equivalently,
  $\exists\varepsilon_0>0$ such that no $\delta>0$ keeps $f$ within
  $\varepsilon_0$ of $f(a)$ on all of $U_\delta(a) \cap E$.
nodes.hint:
The negation of continuity at $a$ — a fixed output gap
  that cannot be eliminated by shrinking the input window.

statement.plain:
A point $a \in E$ where $f$ is not continuous. Equivalently,

nodes.name: def:relative-neighborhood
nodes.question: Define the relative neighborhood of $a$ in $E$.
nodes.statement_display:
$U_E(a) = E \cap U(a)$ where $U(a)$ is a neighborhood of $a$
  in $\mathbb{R}$. It is the part of $E$ within a full neighborhood
  of $a$.
nodes.statement_tex:

  Used to state local properties precisely on arbitrary subsets.

nodes.name: def:removable-discontinuity
nodes.question: What is a removable discontinuity?
nodes.statement_display:
A discontinuity at $a$ that can be repaired by redefining
  $f$ at $a$ alone. The two-sided limit exists but does not equal
  $f(a)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
A discontinuity at $a$ that can be repaired by redefining
  $f$ at $a$ alone. The two-sided limit exists but does not equal
  $f(a)$.
nodes.hint:
The graph has the right limiting behavior but the wrong
  point value. One point can fix it.

nodes.name: def:uniform-continuity
nodes.question: State the definition of uniform continuity of $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_display:
$\forall\varepsilon>0,\,\exists\delta>0,\,\forall x,u \in A:\,
  |x-u|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(u)|<\varepsilon$, where $\delta$
  depends only on $\varepsilon$, not on $x$ or $u$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\forall\varepsilon>0,\,\exists\delta>0,\,\forall x,u \in A:\,
  |x-u|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(u)|<\varepsilon$, where $\delta$
  depends only on $\varepsilon$, not on $x$ or $u$.
nodes.hint:
The quantifier order is the same as continuity, but $\delta$
  comes before the universal over $x, u$ — it cannot depend on the
  base point.
nodes.negation_display:
$f(x)=1/x$ on $(0,1)$: for small $\varepsilon$, any
  $\delta$ fails near $0$ since the function oscillates with
  unbounded slope.

statement.plain:
$\forall\varepsilon>0,\,\exists\delta>0,\,\forall x,u \in A:\,
  |x-u|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(u)|<\varepsilon$, where $\delta$
  depends only on $\varepsilon$, not on $x$ or $u$.
