nodes.name: Order Theorem for Infinite Limits
nodes.question: State the theorem: Order Theorem for Infinite Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in \mathbb{R}\) be a cluster point of \(A\). Suppose that \[ f(x)\le g(x)\qquad\text{for all } x\in A,\ x\ne c. \] Then:
  1. If \[ \lim_{x\to c} f(x)=\infty, \] then \[ \lim_{x\to c} g(x)=\infty. \]
  2. If \[ \lim_{x\to c} g(x)=-\infty, \] then \[ \lim_{x\to c} f(x)=-\infty. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:order-theorem-infinite-limits}
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in \mathbb{R}\) be a cluster point of \(A\).
Suppose that
\[
f(x)\le g(x)\qquad\text{for all } x\in A,\ x\ne c.
\]
Then:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\alph*)}]
\item If
\[
\lim_{x\to c} f(x)=\infty,
\]
then
\[
\lim_{x\to c} g(x)=\infty.
\]

\item If
\[
\lim_{x\to c} g(x)=-\infty,
\]
then
\[
\lim_{x\to c} f(x)=-\infty.
\]
\end{enumerate}

statement.plain:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in \mathbb{R}\) be a cluster point of \(A\). Suppose that \[ f(x)\le g(x)\qquad\text{for all } x\in A,\ x\ne c. \] Then:
  1. If \[ \lim_{x\to c} f(x)=\infty, \] then \[ \lim_{x\to c} g(x)=\infty. \]
  2. If \[ \lim_{x\to c} g(x)=-\infty, \] then \[ \lim_{x\to c} f(x)=-\infty. \]

flash_q.plain: State the theorem: Order Theorem for Infinite Limits.

flash_a.plain:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in \mathbb{R}\) be a cluster point of \(A\). Suppose that \[ f(x)\le g(x)\qquad\text{for all } x\in A,\ x\ne c. \] Then:
  1. If \[ \lim_{x\to c} f(x)=\infty, \] then \[ \lim_{x\to c} g(x)=\infty. \]
  2. If \[ \lim_{x\to c} g(x)=-\infty, \] then \[ \lim_{x\to c} f(x)=-\infty. \]

nodes.statement_display:
$W$ is continuous on $\mathbb{R}$ and differentiable at no point
of $\mathbb{R}$.

nodes.name: Properties of the Cantor function
nodes.question: State the proposition: Properties of the Cantor function.
nodes.statement_display:
$\varphi$ is:
  1. Monotone increasing and maps $[0,1]$ onto $[0,1]$.
  2. Continuous on $[0,1]$ (no jump discontinuities).
  3. Constant on each interval in $[0,1]\setminus C$ (the complement of the Cantor set $C$), i.e., constant on each removed middle-third open interval.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:cantor-function}
$\varphi$ is:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
  \item \textbf{Monotone increasing} and maps $[0,1]$ onto $[0,1]$.
  \item \textbf{Continuous} on $[0,1]$ (no jump discontinuities).
  \item \textbf{Constant} on each interval in $[0,1]\setminus C$
        (the complement of the Cantor set $C$), i.e., constant on
        each removed middle-third open interval.
\end{enumerate}

statement.plain:
$\varphi$ is:
  1. Monotone increasing and maps $[0,1]$ onto $[0,1]$.
  2. Continuous on $[0,1]$ (no jump discontinuities).
  3. Constant on each interval in $[0,1]\setminus C$ (the complement of the Cantor set $C$), i.e., constant on each removed middle-third open interval.

flash_q.plain: State the proposition: Properties of the Cantor function.

flash_a.plain:
$\varphi$ is:

  2. Continuous on $[0,1]$ (no jump discontinuities).

nodes.name: Properties of the jump function
nodes.question: State the proposition: Properties of the jump function.
nodes.statement_display:
$J$ is increasing, left-continuous on $I$, continuous on
$I \setminus \{x_1, x_2, …\}$, and has a jump of magnitude $h_n$
at each $x_n$.
Moreover, every bounded left-continuous increasing function $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$
decomposes as
\[
  f = \varphi + J
\]
where $\varphi$ is continuous and increasing and $J$ is a jump function.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:jump-function-properties}
$J$ is increasing, left-continuous on $I$, continuous on
$I \setminus \{x_1, x_2, \ldots\}$, and has a jump of magnitude $h_n$
at each $x_n$.
Moreover, every bounded left-continuous increasing function $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$
decomposes as
\[
  f = \varphi + J
\]
where $\varphi$ is continuous and increasing and $J$ is a jump function.

nodes.name: Quotient Comparison for Infinite Growth
nodes.question: What does ``$P(f(x))$ is true near $+\infty$'' mean?
nodes.statement_display: There exists $A$ such that $P(f(x))$ holds for all $x > A$.
nodes.statement_tex: There exists $A$ such that $P(f(x))$ holds for all $x > A$.
nodes.hint:
It is the ``sufficiently large $x$'' analogue of the
punctured $\delta$-ball condition for finite $x_0$.

statement.plain: There exists $A$ such that $P(f(x))$ holds for all $x > A$.

flash_q.plain: What does ``$P(f(x))$ is true near $+\infty$'' mean?

flash_a.plain: There exists $A$ such that $P(f(x))$ holds for all $x > A$.

nodes.name: Restriction Locality
nodes.question: When does restricting the domain preserve the limit?
nodes.statement_display:
When $A$ and $S$ agree near $c$: $(A\setminus\{c\})\cap
  B_\alpha(c) = (S\setminus\{c\})\cap B_\alpha(c)$ for some
  $\alpha>0$. Then $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L \iff \lim_{x\to
  c}f|_A(x)=L$.
nodes.statement_tex:
When $A$ and $S$ agree near $c$: $(A\setminus\{c\})\cap
  B_\alpha(c) = (S\setminus\{c\})\cap B_\alpha(c)$ for some
  $\alpha>0$. Then $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L \iff \lim_{x\to
  c}f|_A(x)=L$.
nodes.hint:
Agreement near $c$ means the two domains have the same
  witnesses for any $\delta < \alpha$.
nodes.negation_display:
One-sided limits violate this: $A=(c,\infty)$ and
  $S=\mathbb{R}$ do not agree near $c$ since the left side is
  missing from $A$.

statement.plain:
When $A$ and $S$ agree near $c$: $(A\setminus\{c\})\cap
  B_\alpha(c) = (S\setminus\{c\})\cap B_\alpha(c)$ for some
  $\alpha>0$. Then $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L \iff \lim_{x\to
  c}f|_A(x)=L$.

negation.plain:
One-sided limits violate this: $A=(c,\infty)$ and
  $S=\mathbb{R}$ do not agree near $c$ since the left side is
  missing from $A$.

flash_q.plain: When does restricting the domain preserve the limit?

nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:one-sided-limits}
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in \mathbb{R}\).

\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
\item Suppose \(c\) is a cluster point of \(A \cap (c,\infty)\).
We say that \(L \in \mathbb{R}\) is the \emph{right-hand limit} of \(f\) at \(c\), and write
\[
\lim_{x \to c^+} f(x)=L,
\]
if
\[
\forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in A:
\quad
0<x-c<\delta \;\Rightarrow\; |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon.
\]

\item Suppose \(c\) is a cluster point of \(A \cap (-\infty,c)\).
We say that \(L \in \mathbb{R}\) is the \emph{left-hand limit} of \(f\) at \(c\), and write
\[
\lim_{x \to c^-} f(x)=L,
\]
if
\[
\forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in A:
\quad
0<c-x<\delta \;\Rightarrow\; |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon.
\]
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Sequential Criterion for Infinite Limits as \(x\to\infty\)
nodes.question: State the theorem: Sequential Criterion for Infinite Limits as \(x\to\infty\).
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and suppose that \[ (a,\infty)\subseteq A \] for some \(a\in\mathbb{R}\). Then the following are equivalent:
  1. \[ \lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=\infty \qquad [\text{respectively, } \lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=-\infty]. \]
  2. For every sequence \((x_n)\) in \((a,\infty)\) such that \[ x_n\to\infty, \] we have \[ f(x_n)\to\infty \qquad [\text{respectively, } f(x_n)\to-\infty]. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:sequential-criterion-infinite-limit-at-infinity}
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and suppose that
\[
(a,\infty)\subseteq A
\]
for some \(a\in\mathbb{R}\).
Then the following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
\item
\[
\lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=\infty
\qquad
[\text{respectively, } \lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=-\infty].
\]
\item For every sequence \((x_n)\) in \((a,\infty)\) such that
\[
x_n\to\infty,
\]
we have
\[
f(x_n)\to\infty
\qquad
[\text{respectively, } f(x_n)\to-\infty].
\]
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Sequential Criterion for Left-Hand Limits
nodes.question: State the corollary: Sequential Criterion for Left-Hand Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in \mathbb{R}$ be a cluster point of $A \cap (-\infty, c)$. Then the following are equivalent:
  1. $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to c^-} f(x) = L$.
  2. For every sequence $(x_n)$ such that \[ x_n \in A, \qquad x_n < c \text{ for all } n, \qquad x_n \to c, \] the image sequence $(f(x_n))$ converges to $L$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:sequential-criterion-left-hand-limit}
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let
$c \in \mathbb{R}$ be a cluster point of $A \cap (-\infty, c)$.
Then the following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
  \item $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to c^-} f(x) = L$.
  \item For every sequence $(x_n)$ such that
        \[
          x_n \in A, \qquad x_n < c \text{ for all } n, \qquad x_n \to c,
        \]
        the image sequence $(f(x_n))$ converges to $L$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. ($\Rightarrow$) Fix $\varepsilon$; get $\delta$; use $x_n \to c$ and
     $x_n < c$ to find $N_0$ with $0 < c - x_n < \delta$; conclude
     $|f(x_n) - L| < \varepsilon$. \\
  2. ($\Leftarrow$) Negate limit: fix $\varepsilon_0$; for each $n$ pick
     $x_n \in A$ with $0 < c - x_n < 1/n$ and $|f(x_n) - L| \geq
     \varepsilon_0$. \\
  3. Archimedean $N_1 > 1/\varepsilon$ gives $x_n \to c$; sequence witnesses
     failure of the sequential condition.

statement.plain:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in \mathbb{R}$ be a cluster point of $A \cap (-\infty, c)$. Then the following are equivalent:

  2. For every sequence $(x_n)$ such that \[ x_n \in A, \qquad x_n < c \text{ for all } n, \qquad x_n \to c, \] the image sequence $(f(x_n))$ converges to $L$.

nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:sequential-criterion-right-hand-limit}
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let

nodes.name: Sequential Definition of Limit
nodes.question:
State the sequential definition of
  $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$.
nodes.statement_display:
For every sequence $(x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$
  with $x_n \to c$, the sequence $f(x_n) \to L$.
nodes.statement_tex:
For every sequence $(x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$
  with $x_n \to c$, the sequence $f(x_n) \to L$.
nodes.hint:
Equivalent to the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition
  by the Sequential Criterion. The condition $x_n \neq c$ mirrors
  the $0 <$ in $0 < |x_n - c| < \delta$.
nodes.negation_display:
The sequence must lie in $A \setminus \{c\}$:
  a constant sequence $x_n = c$ is excluded, just as
  $x = c$ is excluded in the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ form.

nodes.name: Signum Function
nodes.question: State the definition: Signum Function.
nodes.statement_display: The signum (or sign) function $\operatorname{sgn} : \mathbb{R} \to \{-1,0,1\}$ is \[ \operatorname{sgn}(x) := \begin{cases} -1 & x < 0 \\ 0 & x = 0 \\ 1 & x > 0. \end{cases} \]

nodes.name: Squeeze Theorem for Function Limits
nodes.question: State the theorem: Squeeze Theorem for Function Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f, g, h : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c$ be a
cluster point of $A$. Suppose that
\[
\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L = \lim_{x \to c} g(x).
\]
If there exists $\delta_0 > 0$ such that
\[
\forall x \in A,\quad 0 < |x - c| < \delta_0 \implies f(x) \leq h(x) \leq g(x),
\]
then $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to c} h(x) = L$.

\begin{remark*}[Fully quantified form]
\[
\Bigl(\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L=\lim_{x\to c}g(x)\Bigr)
\;\wedge\;
\Bigl(\exists\delta_0>0,\;\forall x\in A,\;
0<|x-c|<\delta_0\implies f(x)\leq h(x)\leq g(x)\Bigr)
\implies \lim_{x\to c}h(x)=L.
\]
\end{remark*}

\begin{remark*}[Relationship to sequential version]
This is the function-limit analogue of the Squeeze Theorem for sequences.
The proof routes entirely through the Sequential Criterion: translate to
sequences, apply the sequential squeeze, translate back. No new $\varepsilon$-$\delta$
work is needed.
\end{remark*}

\begin{remark*}[Flash]\end{remark*}

\begin{remark*}[Proof link]
Proof: Squeeze Theorem for Function Limits
\end{remark*}

nodes.name: Supremum and Infimum of a Function
nodes.question: What do $\sup_A f$ and $\inf_A f$ mean?
nodes.statement_display:
$\sup_A f = \sup\{f(x):x\in A\}$ and
$\inf_A f = \inf\{f(x):x\in A\}$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\sup_A f = \sup\{f(x):x\in A\}$ and
$\inf_A f = \inf\{f(x):x\in A\}$.
nodes.hint: They are the supremum and infimum of the range $f(A)$.

nodes.name: Three Equivalent Forms of the Function Limit
nodes.question: State the corollary: Three Equivalent Forms of the Function Limit.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. The following are equivalent:
  1. $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$ \hfill ($\varepsilon$-$\delta$)
  2. For every $V_\varepsilon(L)$ there exists $V_\delta(c)$ such that $x \in V_\delta^*(c) \cap A$ implies $f(x) \in V_\varepsilon(L)$. \hfill (neighbourhood)
  3. For every $(x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$ with $x_n \to c$, $f(x_n) \to L$. \hfill (sequential)
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{cor:limit-three-equiv}
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
The following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$ \hfill ($\varepsilon$-$\delta$)
  \item For every $V_\varepsilon(L)$ there exists $V_\delta(c)$ such
        that $x \in V_\delta^*(c) \cap A$ implies
        $f(x) \in V_\varepsilon(L)$. \hfill (neighbourhood)
  \item For every $(x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$ with
        $x_n \to c$, $f(x_n) \to L$. \hfill (sequential)
\end{enumerate}
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. (i)$\iff$(ii) by Neighbourhood Form proposition.
2. (i)$\iff$(iii) by Sequential Criterion proposition.
3. Transitivity gives all three equivalent.

nodes.name: Two-Sided Limit via One-Sided Limits
nodes.question: State the theorem: Two-Sided Limit via One-Sided Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in \mathbb{R}$
be a cluster point of both
\[
A \cap (c,\infty)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
A \cap (-\infty,c).
\]
Then
\[
\lim_{x\to c} f(x)=L
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\lim_{x\to c^+} f(x)=L=\lim_{x\to c^-} f(x).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:two-sided-limit-iff-matching-one-sided-limits}
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in \mathbb{R}$
be a cluster point of both
\[
A \cap (c,\infty)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
A \cap (-\infty,c).
\]
Then
\[
\lim_{x\to c} f(x)=L
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\lim_{x\to c^+} f(x)=L=\lim_{x\to c^-} f(x).
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. $(\Rightarrow)$: two-sided $\delta$ restricts to each side directly.\\
  2. $(\Leftarrow)$: set $\delta = \min\{\delta_+, \delta_-\}$.\\
  3. Split on $x > c$ and $x < c$; apply respective one-sided hypothesis.\\
  4. Conclude $|f(x) - L| < \varepsilon$ in both cases.

statement.plain:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in \mathbb{R}$
be a cluster point of both
\[
A \cap (c,\infty)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
A \cap (-\infty,c).
\]
Then
\[
\lim_{x\to c} f(x)=L
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\lim_{x\to c^+} f(x)=L=\lim_{x\to c^-} f(x).
\]

flash_q.plain: State the theorem: Two-Sided Limit via One-Sided Limits.

be a cluster point of both
\[

\]

\[

\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad

\]

nodes.name: Uniqueness of Limits of Functions
nodes.question: Why can a function have at most one limit at a cluster point?
nodes.statement_display:
Assume $\lim f = L$ and $\lim f = L'$. Set
  $\varepsilon = |L-L'|/2$. Invoke each limit definition to get
  $\delta_1$, $\delta_2$; take $\delta = \min(\delta_1,\delta_2)$.
  Triangle inequality gives $|L-L'| < |L-L'|$, contradiction.
nodes.statement_tex:
Assume $\lim f = L$ and $\lim f = L'$. Set
  $\varepsilon = |L-L'|/2$. Invoke each limit definition to get
  $\delta_1$, $\delta_2$; take $\delta = \min(\delta_1,\delta_2)$.
  Triangle inequality gives $|L-L'| < |L-L'|$, contradiction.
nodes.hint:
The key pattern: invoke the limit definition twice,
  once for each candidate, then reconcile with
  $\delta=\min(\delta_1,\delta_2)$.
nodes.formal:
\[
  \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L
  \;\wedge\;
  \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L'
  \;\Rightarrow\; L = L'.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
  \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L
  \;\wedge\;
  \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L'
  \;\Rightarrow\; L = L'.
\]
nodes.negation_display:
Without the cluster point condition the limit can be
  vacuously satisfied by every $L$ (isolated point).
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. Assume $L \neq L'$; set $\varepsilon = |L-L'|/2 > 0$.
2. Invoke limit definition twice to get $\delta_1$, $\delta_2$; set
   $\delta = \min(\delta_1, \delta_2)$.
3. Find $x \in A$ with $0 < |x-c| < \delta$; triangle inequality gives
   $|L-L'| < |L-L'|$, contradiction.

statement.plain:
Assume $\lim f = L$ and $\lim f = L'$. Set
  $\varepsilon = |L-L'|/2$. Invoke each limit definition to get
  $\delta_1$, $\delta_2$; take $\delta = \min(\delta_1,\delta_2)$.
  Triangle inequality gives $|L-L'| < |L-L'|$, contradiction.

formal.plain:
\[
  \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L
  \;\wedge\;
  \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L'
  \;\Rightarrow\; L = L'.
\]

negation.plain:
Without the cluster point condition the limit can be
  vacuously satisfied by every $L$ (isolated point).

  $\delta_1$, $\delta_2$; take $\delta = \min(\delta_1,\delta_2)$.
  Triangle inequality gives $|L-L'| < |L-L'|$, contradiction.

nodes.name: Van der Waerden function
nodes.question: State the definition: Van der Waerden function.
nodes.statement_display: \cite[Theorem~8.3.8]{SohrabBasicRealAnalysis2} Let $f_0(x) := d(x, \mathbb{Z}) = \min_{n\in\mathbb{Z}}|x-n|$ be the distance from $x$ to the nearest integer (a continuous sawtooth with period $1$ and amplitude $1/2$). Define $f_n(x) := 4^{-n} f_0(4^n x)$ for $n \geq 0$. The Van der Waerden function is \[ W(x) := \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} f_n(x) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 4^{-n} f_0(4^n x). \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:van-der-waerden}
\cite[Theorem~8.3.8]{SohrabBasicRealAnalysis2}
Let $f_0(x) := d(x, \mathbb{Z}) = \min_{n\in\mathbb{Z}}|x-n|$
be the distance from $x$ to the nearest integer (a continuous sawtooth
with period $1$ and amplitude $1/2$). Define
$f_n(x) := 4^{-n} f_0(4^n x)$ for $n \geq 0$. The
\emph{Van der Waerden function} is
\[
  W(x) := \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} f_n(x)
         = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 4^{-n} f_0(4^n x).
\]

statement.plain: \cite[Theorem~8.3.8]{SohrabBasicRealAnalysis2} Let $f_0(x) := d(x, \mathbb{Z}) = \min_{n\in\mathbb{Z}}|x-n|$ be the distance from $x$ to the nearest integer (a continuous sawtooth with period $1$ and amplitude $1/2$). Define $f_n(x) := 4^{-n} f_0(4^n x)$ for $n \geq 0$. The Van der Waerden function is \[ W(x) := \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} f_n(x) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 4^{-n} f_0(4^n x). \]

flash_q.plain: State the definition: Van der Waerden function.

flash_a.plain: \cite[Theorem~8.3.8]{SohrabBasicRealAnalysis2} Let $f_0(x) := d(x, \mathbb{Z}) = \min_{n\in\mathbb{Z}}|x-n|$ be the distance from $x$ to the nearest integer (a continuous sawtooth with period $1$ and amplitude $1/2$). Define $f_n(x) := 4^{-n} f_0(4^n x)$ for $n \geq 0$. The Van der Waerden function is \[ W(x) := \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} f_n(x) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 4^{-n} f_0(4^n x). \]

nodes.name: Vertical Asymptote
nodes.question: Define vertical asymptote.
nodes.statement_display:
$x = x_0$ is a vertical asymptote if any one of
$\lim_{x\to x_0}f(x)$, $\lim_{x\to x_0^\pm}f(x)$ is $\pm\infty$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$x = x_0$ is a vertical asymptote if any one of
$\lim_{x\to x_0}f(x)$, $\lim_{x\to x_0^\pm}f(x)$ is $\pm\infty$.
nodes.hint:
Vertical asymptotes describe blow-up at a finite input.
Only one one-sided limit needs to be infinite.
nodes.formal:
$x = x_0$ is a vertical asymptote $\iff$
at least one of $\lim_{x\to x_0} f(x)$,
$\lim_{x\to x_0^-} f(x)$, $\lim_{x\to x_0^+} f(x)$
is $\pm\infty$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$x = x_0$ is a vertical asymptote $\iff$
at least one of $\lim_{x\to x_0} f(x)$,
$\lim_{x\to x_0^-} f(x)$, $\lim_{x\to x_0^+} f(x)$
is $\pm\infty$.
nodes.negation_display:
Both one-sided limits need not be infinite — one suffices.
$f(x)=1/x$ has $\lim_{x\to 0^+}=+\infty$ and $\lim_{x\to 0^-}=-\infty$,
both infinite but with opposite signs.

statement.plain:
$x = x_0$ is a vertical asymptote if any one of
$\lim_{x\to x_0}f(x)$, $\lim_{x\to x_0^\pm}f(x)$ is $\pm\infty$.

formal.plain:

at least one of $\lim_{x\to x_0} f(x)$,
$\lim_{x\to x_0^-} f(x)$, $\lim_{x\to x_0^+} f(x)$
is $\pm\infty$.

negation.plain:
Both one-sided limits need not be infinite — one suffices.
$f(x)=1/x$ has $\lim_{x\to 0^+}=+\infty$ and $\lim_{x\to 0^-}=-\infty$,
both infinite but with opposite signs.

flash_a.plain:
$x = x_0$ is a vertical asymptote if any one of
$\lim_{x\to x_0}f(x)$, $\lim_{x\to x_0^\pm}f(x)$ is $\pm\infty$.

nodes.name: Every Power Dominates the Logarithm
nodes.question: Compare $\ln x$ and $x^r$ as $x \to \infty$ and as $x \to 0^+$.
nodes.statement_display:
$x^r$ dominates: $(\ln x)/x^r \to 0$ as $x \to \infty$;
$x^r \ln x \to 0$ as $x \to 0^+$ (even though $\ln x \to -\infty$).
nodes.statement_tex:
$x^r$ dominates: $(\ln x)/x^r \to 0$ as $x \to \infty$;
$x^r \ln x \to 0$ as $x \to 0^+$ (even though $\ln x \to -\infty$).
nodes.hint: Substitute $x = e^t$ and use exponential dominance.

% TODO

\qquad
$\forall r > 0\; \forall \varepsilon > 0\; \exists \delta > 0:\; 0 < x < \delta \Rightarrow |x^r \ln x| < \varepsilon.$

nodes.name: Fundamental Logarithmic Limit
nodes.question: State the fundamental logarithmic limit.
nodes.hint: $\ln(1+x) \approx x$ near $x = 0$. The derivative of $\ln(1+x)$ at $0$ is $1$.

nodes.name: Natural Logarithm
nodes.question: Define $\ln x$ and state its functional equation.
nodes.statement_display:
$\ln x$ is the inverse of $e^x$, i.e., $e^{\ln x} = x$.
Functional equation: $\ln(xy) = \ln x + \ln y$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\ln x$ is the inverse of $e^x$, i.e., $e^{\ln x} = x$.
Functional equation: $\ln(xy) = \ln x + \ln y$.
nodes.hint:
$\ln$ converts products to sums — it is the bridge from
multiplicative to additive structure.
nodes.negation_display: $\ln 0$ is undefined. $\ln$ is only defined for $x > 0$.

statement.plain:
$\ln x$ is the inverse of $e^x$, i.e., $e^{\ln x} = x$.
Functional equation: $\ln(xy) = \ln x + \ln y$.

negation.plain: $\ln 0$ is undefined. $\ln$ is only defined for $x > 0$.

nodes.name: Exponentiation — Tao 2.3.11
nodes.question: State the definition: Exponentiation — Tao 2.3.11.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $m \in \mathbb{N}$. Exponentiation is defined recursively:
\[
m^0 := 1, \qquad m^{n\pp} := m^n \times m.
\]
In particular, $0^0 := 1$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:exp}
Let $m \in \mathbb{N}$. Exponentiation is defined recursively:
\[
m^0 := 1, \qquad m^{n\pp} := m^n \times m.
\]
In particular, $0^0 := 1$.

statement.plain:
Let $m \in \mathbb{N}$. Exponentiation is defined recursively:
\[
m^0 := 1, \qquad m^{n\pp} := m^n \times m.
\]
In particular, $0^0 := 1$.

Let $m \in \mathbb{N}$. Exponentiation is defined recursively:

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.2 — Commutativity
nodes.question: State the lemma: Tao 2.3.2 — Commutativity.
nodes.statement_display: For all $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $n \times m = m \times n$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{lem:mult-comm}
For all $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $n \times m = m \times n$.

statement.plain: For all $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $n \times m = m \times n$.

flash_a.plain: For all $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $n \times m = m \times n$.

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.3 — No zero divisors
nodes.question: State the lemma: Tao 2.3.3 — No zero divisors.
nodes.statement_display:
For all $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $n \times m = 0$ if and only if
$n = 0$ or $m = 0$.

In particular, if $n$ and $m$ are both positive, then $nm$ is positive.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{lem:no-zero-div}

$n = 0$ or $m = 0$.

In particular, if $n$ and $m$ are both positive, then $nm$ is positive.

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.4 — Distributive law
nodes.question: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.4 — Distributive law.
nodes.statement_display:
For all $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$:
\[
a(b+c) = ab + ac \qquad \text{and} \qquad (b+c)a = ba + ca.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:distrib}
For all $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$:
\[
a(b+c) = ab + ac \qquad \text{and} \qquad (b+c)a = ba + ca.
\]

statement.plain:
For all $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$:
\[

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.5 — Associativity
nodes.question: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.5 — Associativity.
nodes.statement_display: For all $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $(a \times b) \times c = a \times (b \times c)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:mult-assoc}
For all $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $(a \times b) \times c = a \times (b \times c)$.

statement.plain: For all $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $(a \times b) \times c = a \times (b \times c)$.

flash_a.plain: For all $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $(a \times b) \times c = a \times (b \times c)$.

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.6 — Multiplication preserves order
nodes.question: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.6 — Multiplication preserves order.
nodes.statement_display: If $a < b$ and $c$ is positive, then $ac < bc$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:mult-order}
If $a < b$ and $c$ is positive, then $ac < bc$.

statement.plain: If $a < b$ and $c$ is positive, then $ac < bc$.

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.7 — Cancellation for multiplication
nodes.question: State the corollary: Tao 2.3.7 — Cancellation for multiplication.
nodes.statement_display: Let $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$ with $ac = bc$ and $c \neq 0$. Then $a = b$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{cor:mult-cancel}
Let $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$ with $ac = bc$ and $c \neq 0$. Then $a = b$.

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.9 — Euclidean algorithm
nodes.question: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.9 — Euclidean algorithm.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $n \in \mathbb{N}$ and let $q$ be positive. Then there exist
$m, r \in \mathbb{N}$ such that:
\[
0 \leq r < q \qquad \text{and} \qquad n = mq + r.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:euclid}
Let $n \in \mathbb{N}$ and let $q$ be positive. Then there exist
$m, r \in \mathbb{N}$ such that:
\[
0 \leq r < q \qquad \text{and} \qquad n = mq + r.
\]

statement.plain:
Let $n \in \mathbb{N}$ and let $q$ be positive. Then there exist
$m, r \in \mathbb{N}$ such that:
\[
0 \leq r < q \qquad \text{and} \qquad n = mq + r.
\]

nodes.name: Negation of Continuity at a Point
nodes.question: What is the negation of continuity at a point?
nodes.statement_display:
A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is not continuous at \(x_0\in E\) if and only if there exists \(\varepsilon_0>0\) such that for every \(\delta>0\), there exists \(x\in E\) with
\[
|x-x_0|<\delta
\quad\text{and}\quad
|f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is not continuous at \(x_0\in E\) if and only if there exists \(\varepsilon_0>0\) such that for every \(\delta>0\), there exists \(x\in E\) with
\[
|x-x_0|<\delta
\quad\text{and}\quad
|f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0.
\]
nodes.hint: Negate the quantifiers in order: \(\forall\exists\forall\) becomes \(\exists\forall\exists\), and the conclusion \(<\varepsilon\) becomes \(\ge \varepsilon\).
nodes.formal:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0\in E\). Then
\[
f \text{ is not continuous at } x_0
\]
if and only if
\[
\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E:
\bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0\in E\). Then
\[
f \text{ is not continuous at } x_0
\]
if and only if
\[
\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E:
\bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr).
\]

statement.plain:
A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is not continuous at \(x_0\in E\) if and only if there exists \(\varepsilon_0>0\) such that for every \(\delta>0\), there exists \(x\in E\) with
\[
|x-x_0|<\delta
\quad\text{and}\quad
|f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0.
\]

formal.plain:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0\in E\). Then
\[
f \text{ is not continuous at } x_0
\]
if and only if
\[
\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E:
\bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr).
\]

flash_q.plain: What is the negation of continuity at a point?

flash_a.plain:
A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is not continuous at \(x_0\in E\) if and only if there exists \(\varepsilon_0>0\) such that for every \(\delta>0\), there exists \(x\in E\) with
\[
|x-x_0|<\delta
\quad\text{and}\quad
|f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0.
\]

nodes.name: $(1 + 1/x)^x \to e$
nodes.question: What is $\lim_{x\to\infty}(1+1/x)^x$?
nodes.hint: Write as $e^{x\ln(1+1/x)}$ and use $\ln(1+t)/t \to 1$.

nodes.name: Exponential Dominates Every Power
nodes.question: Compare $x^r$ and $e^x$ as $x \to \infty$.
nodes.statement_display: $e^x$ dominates: $x^r/e^x \to 0$ for every $r > 0$.
nodes.statement_tex: $e^x$ dominates: $x^r/e^x \to 0$ for every $r > 0$.
nodes.hint: One term of the series $e^x \geq x^n/n!$ is enough to dominate any power.

statement.plain: $e^x$ dominates: $x^r/e^x \to 0$ for every $r > 0$.

flash_a.plain: $e^x$ dominates: $x^r/e^x \to 0$ for every $r > 0$.

nodes.name: Exponential Function $e^x$
nodes.question: Define $e^x$ and state its two key properties.
nodes.statement_display:
$e^x = \sum_{n=0}^\infty x^n/n!$.
Properties: $e^{x+y} = e^xe^y$ and $(e^x)' = e^x$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$e^x = \sum_{n=0}^\infty x^n/n!$.
Properties: $e^{x+y} = e^xe^y$ and $(e^x)' = e^x$.
nodes.hint:
The functional equation converts addition to multiplication.
The self-derivative property is unique to base $e$.
nodes.formal:
$\forall x \in \mathbb{R},\; e^x = \sum_{n=0}^\infty x^n/n!$,
convergent absolutely since $\sum |x|^n/n! < \infty$ by the ratio test.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\forall x \in \mathbb{R},\; e^x = \sum_{n=0}^\infty x^n/n!$,
convergent absolutely since $\sum |x|^n/n! < \infty$ by the ratio test.
nodes.negation_display:
$2^x$ is an exponential but $(2^x)' = 2^x \ln 2 \neq 2^x$.
Only $e^x$ is its own derivative.

statement.plain:
$e^x = \sum_{n=0}^\infty x^n/n!$.
Properties: $e^{x+y} = e^xe^y$ and $(e^x)' = e^x$.

formal.plain:
$\forall x \in \mathbb{R},\; e^x = \sum_{n=0}^\infty x^n/n!$,
convergent absolutely since $\sum |x|^n/n! < \infty$ by the ratio test.

negation.plain:
$2^x$ is an exponential but $(2^x)' = 2^x \ln 2 \neq 2^x$.
Only $e^x$ is its own derivative.

flash_q.plain: Define $e^x$ and state its two key properties.

flash_a.plain:
$e^x = \sum_{n=0}^\infty x^n/n!$.
Properties: $e^{x+y} = e^xe^y$ and $(e^x)' = e^x$.

nodes.name: Fundamental Exponential Limit
nodes.question: State the fundamental exponential limit.
nodes.hint: $e^x \approx 1 + x$ near $x = 0$. This is the first-order Taylor approximation.

nodes.name: Power Function
nodes.question: Define $x^r$ for $r \in \mathbb{R}$, $x > 0$.
nodes.statement_display: $x^r := e^{r\ln x}$.
nodes.statement_tex: $x^r := e^{r\ln x}$.
nodes.hint:
This unifies integer, rational, and irrational exponents into
one definition and makes differentiability immediate.
nodes.formal:
$\forall r \in \mathbb{R},\; \forall x > 0,\; x^r := e^{r\ln x}$.
For $r \in \mathbb{N}$ this reduces to $\underbrace{x \cdot x \cdots x}_r$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\forall r \in \mathbb{R},\; \forall x > 0,\; x^r := e^{r\ln x}$.
For $r \in \mathbb{N}$ this reduces to $\underbrace{x \cdot x \cdots x}_r$.

statement.plain: $x^r := e^{r\ln x}$.

formal.plain:
$\forall r \in \mathbb{R},\; \forall x > 0,\; x^r := e^{r\ln x}$.
For $r \in \mathbb{N}$ this reduces to $\underbrace{x \cdot x \cdots x}_r$.

flash_a.plain: $x^r := e^{r\ln x}$.

nodes.name: The Number $e$
nodes.question: Give two equivalent definitions of $e$.
nodes.statement_display: $e = \sum_{n=0}^\infty 1/n! = \lim_{n\to\infty}(1+1/n)^n$.
nodes.statement_tex: $e = \sum_{n=0}^\infty 1/n! = \lim_{n\to\infty}(1+1/n)^n$.
nodes.hint:
Series definition gives decimal control; limit definition
connects to compound interest.

statement.plain: $e = \sum_{n=0}^\infty 1/n! = \lim_{n\to\infty}(1+1/n)^n$.

flash_a.plain: $e = \sum_{n=0}^\infty 1/n! = \lim_{n\to\infty}(1+1/n)^n$.

nodes.name: Predicate Substitution under Equality
nodes.question: State the theorem: Predicate Substitution under Equality.
nodes.statement_display:
If $t_1 = t_2$ and $P$ is an $n$-ary predicate symbol, then
$P(…,t_1,…) \;\Leftrightarrow\; P(…,t_2,…)$.
nodes.formal: $\forall t_1, t_2:\; t_1=t_2 \to \forall$ predicate symbol $P$: $P(…,t_1,…) \leftrightarrow P(…,t_2,…)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall t_1, t_2:\; t_1=t_2 \to \forall$ predicate symbol $P$: $P(…,t_1,…) \leftrightarrow P(…,t_2,…)$.

statement.plain:
If $t_1 = t_2$ and $P$ is an $n$-ary predicate symbol, then
$P(…,t_1,…) \;\Leftrightarrow\; P(…,t_2,…)$.

formal.plain: $\forall t_1, t_2:\; t_1=t_2 \to \forall$ predicate symbol $P$: $P(…,t_1,…) \leftrightarrow P(…,t_2,…)$.

flash_q.plain: State the theorem: Predicate Substitution under Equality.

flash_a.plain:
If $t_1 = t_2$ and $P$ is an $n$-ary predicate symbol, then
$P(…,t_1,…) \;\Leftrightarrow\; P(…,t_2,…)$.

nodes.name: Soundness of First-Order Logic
nodes.question: State the theorem: Soundness of First-Order Logic.
nodes.statement_display:
The standard inference rules for first-order logic (UI, UG, EI, EG, and the
propositional rules) are sound: they preserve truth in all structures.

If $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$, then $\Gamma \models \varphi$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:fol-sound}
The standard inference rules for first-order logic (UI, UG, EI, EG, and the
propositional rules) are sound: they preserve truth in all structures.

If $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$, then $\Gamma \models \varphi$.

statement.plain:
The standard inference rules for first-order logic (UI, UG, EI, EG, and the
propositional rules) are sound: they preserve truth in all structures.

If $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$, then $\Gamma \models \varphi$.

nodes.name: Symmetry of Equality
nodes.question: State the theorem: Symmetry of Equality.
nodes.statement_display: From $t_1 = t_2$, one may infer $t_2 = t_1$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:eq-sym}
From $t_1 = t_2$, one may infer $t_2 = t_1$.

statement.plain: From $t_1 = t_2$, one may infer $t_2 = t_1$.

flash_q.plain: State the theorem: Symmetry of Equality.

flash_a.plain: From $t_1 = t_2$, one may infer $t_2 = t_1$.

nodes.name: Term Substitution under Equality
nodes.question: State the theorem: Term Substitution under Equality.
nodes.statement_display:
If $t_1 = t_2$, then for any function symbol $f$,
$f(…,t_1,…) = f(…,t_2,…)$.
nodes.formal: $\forall t_1, t_2:\; t_1=t_2 \to \forall$ function symbol $f$: $f(…,t_1,…) = f(…,t_2,…)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall t_1, t_2:\; t_1=t_2 \to \forall$ function symbol $f$: $f(…,t_1,…) = f(…,t_2,…)$.

statement.plain:
If $t_1 = t_2$, then for any function symbol $f$,
$f(…,t_1,…) = f(…,t_2,…)$.

formal.plain: $\forall t_1, t_2:\; t_1=t_2 \to \forall$ function symbol $f$: $f(…,t_1,…) = f(…,t_2,…)$.

flash_q.plain: State the theorem: Term Substitution under Equality.

flash_a.plain:
If $t_1 = t_2$, then for any function symbol $f$,
$f(…,t_1,…) = f(…,t_2,…)$.

nodes.name: Transitivity of Equality
nodes.question: State the theorem: Transitivity of Equality.
nodes.statement_display: From $t_1 = t_2$ and $t_2 = t_3$, one may infer $t_1 = t_3$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:eq-trans}
From $t_1 = t_2$ and $t_2 = t_3$, one may infer $t_1 = t_3$.
nodes.formal: $\forall t_1, t_2, t_3:\; t_1=t_2 \wedge t_2=t_3 \to t_1=t_3$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall t_1, t_2, t_3:\; t_1=t_2 \wedge t_2=t_3 \to t_1=t_3$.

statement.plain: From $t_1 = t_2$ and $t_2 = t_3$, one may infer $t_1 = t_3$.

formal.plain: $\forall t_1, t_2, t_3:\; t_1=t_2 \wedge t_2=t_3 \to t_1=t_3$.

flash_q.plain: State the theorem: Transitivity of Equality.

flash_a.plain: From $t_1 = t_2$ and $t_2 = t_3$, one may infer $t_1 = t_3$.

nodes.name: Negation of Bounded Quantifiers
nodes.question: State the theorem: Negation of Bounded Quantifiers.
nodes.formal:
$
eg( orall x\,(x\in A 	o \varphi)) \;\equiv\; \exists x\,(x\in A \wedge 
eg\varphi)$, and $
eg(\exists x\,(x\in A \wedge \varphi)) \;\equiv\;  orall x\,(x\in A 	o 
eg\varphi)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$
eg( orall x\,(x\in A 	o \varphi)) \;\equiv\; \exists x\,(x\in A \wedge 
eg\varphi)$, and $
eg(\exists x\,(x\in A \wedge \varphi)) \;\equiv\;  orall x\,(x\in A 	o 
eg\varphi)$.

formal.plain:
$
eg( orall x\,(x\in A 	o \varphi)) \;\equiv\; \exists x\,(x\in A \wedge 
eg\varphi)$, and $
eg(\exists x\,(x\in A \wedge \varphi)) \;\equiv\;  orall x\,(x\in A 	o 
eg\varphi)$.

nodes.name: Quantifier Commutation
nodes.question: State the proposition: Quantifier Commutation.
nodes.statement_display: Quantifiers of the same type commute: \begin{align*} \forall x\,\forall y\,\varphi &\;\equiv\; \forall y\,\forall x\,\varphi, \\ \exists x\,\exists y\,\varphi &\;\equiv\; \exists y\,\exists x\,\varphi. \end{align*} However, quantifiers of different types do not commute in general: \[ \forall x\,\exists y\,\varphi \;\not\equiv\; \exists y\,\forall x\,\varphi. \]

statement.plain: Quantifiers of the same type commute: \begin{align*} \forall x\,\forall y\,\varphi &\;\equiv\; \forall y\,\forall x\,\varphi, \\ \exists x\,\exists y\,\varphi &\;\equiv\; \exists y\,\exists x\,\varphi. \end{align*} However, quantifiers of different types do not commute in general: \[ \forall x\,\exists y\,\varphi \;\not\equiv\; \exists y\,\forall x\,\varphi. \]

nodes.name: Substitution Lemma for Formulas
nodes.question: State the lemma: Substitution Lemma for Formulas.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\varphi$ be a formula, $t$ a term free for $x$ in $\varphi$. Then for
any structure $\mathcal{M}$ and assignment $s$,
\[
\mathcal{M},s \models \varphi[t/x]
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal{M},\,s\bigl[x\mapsto \llbracket t \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},s}\bigr] \models \varphi.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{lem:subst-formulas}
Let $\varphi$ be a formula, $t$ a term free for $x$ in $\varphi$. Then for
any structure $\mathcal{M}$ and assignment $s$,
\[
\mathcal{M},s \models \varphi[t/x]
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal{M},\,s\bigl[x\mapsto \llbracket t \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},s}\bigr] \models \varphi.
\]

statement.plain:
Let $\varphi$ be a formula, $t$ a term free for $x$ in $\varphi$. Then for
any structure $\mathcal{M}$ and assignment $s$,
\[
\mathcal{M},s \models \varphi[t/x]
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal{M},\,s\bigl[x\mapsto \llbracket t \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},s}\bigr] \models \varphi.
\]

flash_q.plain: State the lemma: Substitution Lemma for Formulas.

flash_a.plain:
Let $\varphi$ be a formula, $t$ a term free for $x$ in $\varphi$. Then for
any structure $\mathcal{M}$ and assignment $s$,
\[
\mathcal{M},s \models \varphi[t/x]
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal{M},\,s\bigl[x\mapsto \llbracket t \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},s}\bigr] \models \varphi.
\]

nodes.name: Substitution Lemma for Terms
nodes.question: State the lemma: Substitution Lemma for Terms.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\mathcal{M}$ be a structure, $s$ a variable assignment, $x$ a variable,
and $d \in D$. For any term $t$,
\[
\llbracket t \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},\,s[x\mapsto d]}
=
\llbracket t[d/x] \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},\,s}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{lem:subst-terms}
Let $\mathcal{M}$ be a structure, $s$ a variable assignment, $x$ a variable,
and $d \in D$. For any term $t$,
\[
\llbracket t \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},\,s[x\mapsto d]}
=
\llbracket t[d/x] \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},\,s}.
\]

statement.plain:
Let $\mathcal{M}$ be a structure, $s$ a variable assignment, $x$ a variable,
and $d \in D$. For any term $t$,
\[
\llbracket t \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},\,s[x\mapsto d]}
=
\llbracket t[d/x] \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},\,s}.
\]

flash_q.plain: State the lemma: Substitution Lemma for Terms.

flash_a.plain:
Let $\mathcal{M}$ be a structure, $s$ a variable assignment, $x$ a variable,
and $d \in D$. For any term $t$,
\[
\llbracket t \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},\,s[x\mapsto d]}
=
\llbracket t[d/x] \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},\,s}.
\]

nodes.name: $\varepsilon$-characterisation of $\sup$
nodes.question: State the $\varepsilon$-characterisation of $\sup$ (proof-techniques version).
nodes.statement_display: If $s=\sup S$ then $\forall\varepsilon>0\,\exists x\in S: x>s-\varepsilon$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $s=\sup S$ then $\forall\varepsilon>0\,\exists x\in S: x>s-\varepsilon$.

statement.plain: If $s=\sup S$ then $\forall\varepsilon>0\,\exists x\in S: x>s-\varepsilon$.

flash_a.plain: If $s=\sup S$ then $\forall\varepsilon>0\,\exists x\in S: x>s-\varepsilon$.

nodes.name: Bolzano--Weierstrass via bisection
nodes.question: State Bolzano–Weierstrass.
nodes.statement_display: Every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a convergent subsequence.
nodes.statement_tex: Every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a convergent subsequence.
nodes.hint: Extract a monotone subsequence; bounded monotone sequences converge by MCT.

statement.plain: Every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a convergent subsequence.

flash_a.plain: Every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a convergent subsequence.

nodes.name: Convergent sequences are bounded
nodes.question: State: convergent sequences are bounded.
nodes.statement_display: If $(b_n)\to b$ then $\exists M>0$ with $|b_n|\le M$ for all $n$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $(b_n)\to b$ then $\exists M>0$ with $|b_n|\le M$ for all $n$.
nodes.hint: Take $\varepsilon=1$: beyond $N$ all terms lie in $(b-1,b+1)$; finitely many terms before $N$ give a finite bound.

statement.plain: If $(b_n)\to b$ then $\exists M>0$ with $|b_n|\le M$ for all $n$.

flash_a.plain: If $(b_n)\to b$ then $\exists M>0$ with $|b_n|\le M$ for all $n$.

nodes.name: Intermediate Value Theorem
nodes.question: State the Intermediate Value Theorem.
nodes.statement_display: If $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous and $f(a)<L<f(b)$ (or $f(a)>L>f(b)$), then $\exists c\in(a,b): f(c)=L$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous and $f(a)<L<f(b)$ (or $f(a)>L>f(b)$), then $\exists c\in(a,b): f(c)=L$.
nodes.hint: Bisection: maintain $f(a_n)<L<f(b_n)$; the intersection point $c$ satisfies $f(c)=L$ by continuity.

statement.plain: If $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous and $f(a)<L<f(b)$ (or $f(a)>L>f(b)$), then $\exists c\in(a,b): f(c)=L$.

flash_a.plain: If $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous and $f(a)<L<f(b)$ (or $f(a)>L>f(b)$), then $\exists c\in(a,b): f(c)=L$.

nodes.name: Monotone Approximation of Bounds
nodes.question: State Monotone Approximation of Bounds (proof-techniques version).
nodes.statement_display: For nonempty bounded $S$: there exist monotone sequences in $S$ converging to $\sup S$ and $\inf S$.
nodes.statement_tex: For nonempty bounded $S$: there exist monotone sequences in $S$ converging to $\sup S$ and $\inf S$.

statement.plain: For nonempty bounded $S$: there exist monotone sequences in $S$ converging to $\sup S$ and $\inf S$.

flash_q.plain: State Monotone Approximation of Bounds (proof-techniques version).

flash_a.plain: For nonempty bounded $S$: there exist monotone sequences in $S$ converging to $\sup S$ and $\inf S$.

nodes.name: Order arithmetic
nodes.question: State the order arithmetic properties of $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_display: (i) $a\le b,c\le d\Rightarrow a+c\le b+d$; (ii) $a\le b,c>0\Rightarrow ac\le bc$; (iii) $a\le b,c<0\Rightarrow ac\ge bc$.
nodes.statement_tex: (i) $a\le b,c\le d\Rightarrow a+c\le b+d$; (ii) $a\le b,c>0\Rightarrow ac\le bc$; (iii) $a\le b,c<0\Rightarrow ac\ge bc$.
nodes.hint: Multiplication by a negative flips the inequality.

statement.plain: (i) $a\le b,c\le d\Rightarrow a+c\le b+d$; (ii) $a\le b,c>0\Rightarrow ac\le bc$; (iii) $a\le b,c<0\Rightarrow ac\ge bc$.

flash_q.plain: State the order arithmetic properties of $\mathbb{R}$.

flash_a.plain: (i) $a\le b,c\le d\Rightarrow a+c\le b+d$; (ii) $a\le b,c>0\Rightarrow ac\le bc$; (iii) $a\le b,c<0\Rightarrow ac\ge bc$.

nodes.name: Residue divergence criterion
nodes.question: State the Residue Divergence Criterion.
nodes.statement_display: If residue subsequences $(a_{kn+r})\to L$ and $(a_{kn+s})\to M$ with $L\neq M$, then $(a_n)$ diverges.
nodes.statement_tex: If residue subsequences $(a_{kn+r})\to L$ and $(a_{kn+s})\to M$ with $L\neq M$, then $(a_n)$ diverges.

statement.plain: If residue subsequences $(a_{kn+r})\to L$ and $(a_{kn+s})\to M$ with $L\neq M$, then $(a_n)$ diverges.

flash_a.plain: If residue subsequences $(a_{kn+r})\to L$ and $(a_{kn+s})\to M$ with $L\neq M$, then $(a_n)$ diverges.

nodes.name: The dichotomy
nodes.question: State the IO/EV dichotomy.
nodes.statement_display: For any predicate $P$: exactly one of ($P$ infinitely often) or ($\neg P$ eventually) holds.
nodes.statement_tex: For any predicate $P$: exactly one of ($P$ infinitely often) or ($\neg P$ eventually) holds.

statement.plain: For any predicate $P$: exactly one of ($P$ infinitely often) or ($\neg P$ eventually) holds.

flash_a.plain: For any predicate $P$: exactly one of ($P$ infinitely often) or ($\neg P$ eventually) holds.

nodes.name: prop:r-uncountable
nodes.question: State: $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable.
nodes.statement_display: $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable: any proposed enumeration can be contradicted by a nested-interval construction.
nodes.statement_tex: $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable: any proposed enumeration can be contradicted by a nested-interval construction.

statement.plain: $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable: any proposed enumeration can be contradicted by a nested-interval construction.

flash_a.plain: $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable: any proposed enumeration can be contradicted by a nested-interval construction.

nodes.name: Compactness of Logical Consequence
nodes.question: State the corollary: Compactness of Logical Consequence.
nodes.statement_display: If $\Gamma \models \varphi$, then there exists a finite subset $\Gamma_0 \subseteq \Gamma$ with $\Gamma_0 \models \varphi$. Equivalently: logical consequence from an infinite premise set always depends on only finitely many premises.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{cor:consequence-compact}
If $\Gamma \models \varphi$, then there exists a \emph{finite} subset
$\Gamma_0 \subseteq \Gamma$ with $\Gamma_0 \models \varphi$.

Equivalently: logical consequence from an infinite premise set always depends
on only finitely many premises.

statement.plain: If $\Gamma \models \varphi$, then there exists a finite subset $\Gamma_0 \subseteq \Gamma$ with $\Gamma_0 \models \varphi$. Equivalently: logical consequence from an infinite premise set always depends on only finitely many premises.

flash_q.plain: State the corollary: Compactness of Logical Consequence.

flash_a.plain: If $\Gamma \models \varphi$, then there exists a finite subset $\Gamma_0 \subseteq \Gamma$ with $\Gamma_0 \models \varphi$. Equivalently: logical consequence from an infinite premise set always depends on only finitely many premises.

nodes.name: Conditional Introduction (Conditional Proof)
nodes.question: State the definition: Conditional Introduction (Conditional Proof).
nodes.statement_display:
If assuming $P$ in a subproof leads to $Q$, then discharge the assumption and
infer $P \rightarrow Q$:
\[
\frac{[P \vdash Q]}{P \rightarrow Q}
\]

infer $P \rightarrow Q$:

nodes.name: Disjunction Elimination (Proof by Cases)
nodes.question: State the definition: Disjunction Elimination (Proof by Cases).
nodes.statement_display:
From a disjunction, if the same conclusion $R$ follows from each disjunct, infer $R$:
\[
\frac{P \vee Q \qquad [P \vdash R] \qquad [Q \vdash R]}{R}
\]

nodes.name: Addition and multiplication
nodes.question: State the definition: Addition and multiplication.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $[(a,b)],[(c,d)]\in\mathbb{Q}$, where $(a,b),(c,d)\in W$.  Define
\[
[(a,b)] + [(c,d)] := [(ad+bc,bd)]
\]
and
\[
[(a,b)]\cdot[(c,d)] := [(ac,bd)].
\]

nodes.name: Archimedean Property
nodes.question: State the definition: Archimedean Property.
nodes.statement_display: An ordered field $F$ is said to satisfy the Archimedean property if for every element $x \in F$ there exists a natural number $n$ such that \[ n > x. \]
nodes.statement_tex:

if for every element $x \in F$ there exists a natural number $n$ such

\[
n > x.
\]

nodes.name: Archimedean property of $\mathbb{Q}$
nodes.question: State the theorem: Archimedean property of $\mathbb{Q}$.
nodes.statement_display:
For every $x\in\mathbb{Q}$ there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
n>x.
\]
Equivalently, for every $x>0$ there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
\frac{1}{n}<x.
\]

nodes.name: Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{Q}$
nodes.question: State the definition: Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{Q}$.
nodes.statement_display: A sequence $(x_n)$ of rational numbers is called a Cauchy sequence if \[ \forall \varepsilon>0\ \exists N\in\mathbb{N}\ \forall m,n\ge N, \qquad |x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:cauchy-rational}
A sequence $(x_n)$ of rational numbers is called a \emph{Cauchy sequence} if
\[
\forall \varepsilon>0\ \exists N\in\mathbb{N}\ \forall m,n\ge N,
\qquad
|x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon.
\]

statement.plain: A sequence $(x_n)$ of rational numbers is called a Cauchy sequence if \[ \forall \varepsilon>0\ \exists N\in\mathbb{N}\ \forall m,n\ge N, \qquad |x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon. \]

nodes.name: Denominator growth on infinite Stern--Brocot paths
nodes.question: State the lemma: Denominator growth on infinite Stern--Brocot paths.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $a_0/b_0, a_1/b_1, a_2/b_2, …$ be the sequence of fractions
encountered along any infinite path in the Stern–Brocot tree (i.e.\ the
sequence of interval-endpoint fractions at successive levels). Then
\[
  b_0 < b_1 < b_2 < \cdots,
\]
and in particular $b_n \to \infty$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{lem:sb-denom-growth}
Let $a_0/b_0, a_1/b_1, a_2/b_2, \ldots$ be the sequence of fractions
encountered along any infinite path in the Stern--Brocot tree (i.e.\ the
sequence of interval-endpoint fractions at successive levels). Then
\[
  b_0 < b_1 < b_2 < \cdots,
\]
and in particular $b_n \to \infty$.
nodes.formal:
$\forall n \in \mathbb{N},\ b_n < b_{n+1}$.
In particular, $\forall M > 0\ \exists N \in \mathbb{N}\ \forall n \ge N,\ b_n > M$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\forall n \in \mathbb{N},\ b_n < b_{n+1}$.
In particular, $\forall M > 0\ \exists N \in \mathbb{N}\ \forall n \ge N,\ b_n > M$.

statement.plain:
Let $a_0/b_0, a_1/b_1, a_2/b_2, …$ be the sequence of fractions
encountered along any infinite path in the Stern–Brocot tree (i.e.\ the
sequence of interval-endpoint fractions at successive levels). Then
\[
  b_0 < b_1 < b_2 < \cdots,
\]
and in particular $b_n \to \infty$.

formal.plain:
$\forall n \in \mathbb{N},\ b_n < b_{n+1}$.
In particular, $\forall M > 0\ \exists N \in \mathbb{N}\ \forall n \ge N,\ b_n > M$.

flash_q.plain: State the lemma: Denominator growth on infinite Stern–Brocot paths.

nodes.name: Density of $\mathbb{Q}$ in $\mathbb{R}$
nodes.question: State the theorem: Density of $\mathbb{Q}$ in $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_display:
If $x,y\in\mathbb{R}$ and $x<y$, then there exists $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that
\[
x<q<y.
\]

nodes.name: Dirichlet's Approximation Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Dirichlet's Approximation Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
For every real number $x$ there exist infinitely many rational numbers $p/q$ with $p\in\mathbb{Z}$ and
$q\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
\left|x-\frac{p}{q}\right|<\frac{1}{q^2}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:dirichlet-approx}

$q\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
\left|x-\frac{p}{q}\right|<\frac{1}{q^2}.
\]

nodes.name: Equality of fraction pairs
nodes.question: State the definition: Equality of fraction pairs.
nodes.statement_display:
For $(a,b),(c,d)\in W$, define
\[
(a,b) \sim (c,d)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
ad = bc.
\]

In fully quantified form,
\[
\forall (a,b),(c,d)\in W,
\qquad
(a,b)\sim(c,d)
\Longleftrightarrow
ad=bc.
\]

nodes.name: Every infinite Stern--Brocot path converges
nodes.question: State the corollary: Every infinite Stern--Brocot path converges.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $x > 0$ be a real number and let $(I_n)$ be the Stern–Brocot
refinement sequence targeting $x$.  Then the sequence of left endpoints
$(a_n/b_n)$ and the sequence of right endpoints $(c_n/d_n)$ both
converge in $\mathbb{R}$ to a common limit $\xi$, and $\xi = x$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{cor:sb-path-limit}
Let $x > 0$ be a real number and let $(I_n)$ be the Stern--Brocot
refinement sequence targeting $x$.  Then the sequence of left endpoints
$(a_n/b_n)$ and the sequence of right endpoints $(c_n/d_n)$ both
converge in $\mathbb{R}$ to a common limit $\xi$, and $\xi = x$.

nodes.name: Farey neighbor determinant condition
nodes.question: State the theorem: Farey neighbor determinant condition.
nodes.statement_display:
Suppose
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\qquad\text{with}\qquad
\gcd(a,b)=\gcd(c,d)=1,
\quad b,d>0.
\]
If
\[
bc-ad=1,
\]
then the two fractions are neighbors in a Farey sequence, and their mediant is already in reduced
form:
\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:farey-neighbor}

nodes.name: Fraction pairs
nodes.question: State the definition: Fraction pairs.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\mathbb{Z}$ denote the set of integers and define
\[
W = \mathbb{Z} \times (\mathbb{Z}\setminus\{0\}).
\]
Thus
\[
W = \{(a,b) : a,b\in\mathbb{Z},\ b\neq 0\}.
\]

Each ordered pair $(a,b)$ is intended to represent the formal fraction $a/b$.

nodes.name: Gap Between Farey Neighbors
nodes.question: State the theorem: Gap Between Farey Neighbors.
nodes.statement_display:
Let

\[
\frac{a}{b} < \frac{c}{d}
\]

be consecutive fractions in a Farey sequence.

Then

\[
\frac{c}{d} - \frac{a}{b}
=
\frac{1}{bd}.
\]

nodes.name: Infinite Stern--Brocot paths converge to irrationals
nodes.question: State the corollary: Infinite Stern--Brocot paths converge to irrationals.
nodes.statement_display:
A positive real number $x$ corresponds to a finite path in the
Stern–Brocot tree if and only if $x$ is rational.  Equivalently,
$x$ corresponds to an infinite path if and only if $x$ is irrational.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{cor:sb-limit-irrational}
A positive real number $x$ corresponds to a finite path in the
Stern--Brocot tree if and only if $x$ is rational.  Equivalently,
$x$ corresponds to an infinite path if and only if $x$ is irrational.
nodes.formal:
$x \in \mathbb{Q}_{>0}\ \Longleftrightarrow\ $ the Stern–Brocot refinement
process for $x$ terminates in finitely many steps.
Equivalently:
$x \notin \mathbb{Q}\ \Longleftrightarrow\ $ the refinement process
runs for infinitely many steps.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$x \in \mathbb{Q}_{>0}\ \Longleftrightarrow\ $ the Stern–Brocot refinement
process for $x$ terminates in finitely many steps.
Equivalently:
$x \notin \mathbb{Q}\ \Longleftrightarrow\ $ the refinement process
runs for infinitely many steps.

$x$ corresponds to an infinite path if and only if $x$ is irrational.

nodes.name: Mediant
nodes.question: State the definition: Mediant.
nodes.statement_display: Let \[ \frac{a}{b},\frac{c}{d}\in\mathbb{Q}, \qquad b,d>0. \] The mediant of these two fractions is \[ \frac{a+c}{b+d}. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:mediant}

\[
\frac{a}{b},\frac{c}{d}\in\mathbb{Q},
\qquad b,d>0.
\]
The \emph{mediant} of these two fractions is
\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}.
\]

nodes.name: Mediant Gap Lemma
nodes.question: State the lemma: Mediant Gap Lemma.
nodes.statement_display:
If

\[
\frac{a}{b} < \frac{c}{d},
\]

then the mediant

\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}
\]

lies between them, and the gaps satisfy

\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}-\frac{a}{b}
=
\frac{1}{b(b+d)}
\]

and

\[
\frac{c}{d}-\frac{a+c}{b+d}
=
\frac{1}{d(b+d)}.
\]

nodes.name: Nested Stern--Brocot intervals contain a unique limit point
nodes.question: State the proposition: Nested Stern--Brocot intervals contain a unique limit point.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(I_n)_{n \ge 0}$ be the nested sequence of closed rational intervals
generated by the Stern–Brocot refinement process targeting a real number
$x > 0$, with $I_n = [a_n/b_n,\; c_n/d_n]$.  Then there exists a unique
real number $\xi \in \bigcap_{n=0}^{\infty} I_n$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:sb-unique-point}
Let $(I_n)_{n \ge 0}$ be the nested sequence of closed rational intervals
generated by the Stern--Brocot refinement process targeting a real number
$x > 0$, with $I_n = [a_n/b_n,\; c_n/d_n]$.  Then there exists a unique

nodes.name: Positive cone
nodes.question: State the definition: Positive cone.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $F$ be a field. A subset $P\subset F$ is called a positive cone if it satisfies:
  1. if $a,b\in P$, then $a+b\in P$;
  2. if $a,b\in P$, then $ab\in P$;
  3. for every $a\in F$, exactly one of the following holds: \[ a\in P, \qquad a=0, \qquad -a\in P. \]
Define the order relation by \[ a
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:positive-cone}
Let $F$ be a field.  A subset $P\subset F$ is called a \emph{positive cone} if it satisfies:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item if $a,b\in P$, then $a+b\in P$;
  \item if $a,b\in P$, then $ab\in P$;
  \item for every $a\in F$, exactly one of the following holds:
  \[
  a\in P,
  \qquad
  a=0,
  \qquad
  -a\in P.
  \]
\end{enumerate}
Define the order relation by
\[
a<b \iff b-a\in P.
\]

  3. for every $a\in F$, exactly one of the following holds: \[ a\in P, \qquad a=0, \qquad -a\in P. \]

nodes.name: Rational numbers
nodes.question: State the definition: Rational numbers.
nodes.statement_display: The set of rational numbers is the quotient \[ \mathbb{Q} = W/\sim. \] If $(a,b)\in W$, its equivalence class is denoted by \[ [(a,b)]. \] We interpret $[(a,b)]$ as the rational number represented informally by $a/b$.

[(a,b)].

nodes.name: Stern--Brocot interval lengths shrink to zero
nodes.question: State the lemma: Stern--Brocot interval lengths shrink to zero.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(I_n)_{n \ge 0}$ be the sequence of nested rational intervals generated
by the Stern–Brocot refinement process targeting a real number $x > 0$,
where $I_n = [a_n/b_n,\; c_n/d_n]$.  Write $\ell_n = c_n/d_n - a_n/b_n$
for the length of $I_n$.  Then $\ell_n \to 0$.

nodes.name: Stern--Brocot interval refinement
nodes.question: State the theorem: Stern--Brocot interval refinement.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $x>0$ be a real number.  Suppose one begins with two rational
numbers

\[
\frac{a}{b} < x < \frac{c}{d},
\qquad b,d>0,
\]

and repeatedly inserts the mediant

\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}.
\]

At each step, replace one endpoint by the mediant so that the new
interval still contains $x$.  Then this process generates a nested
sequence of rational intervals containing $x$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:stern-brocot-refinement}

numbers

\qquad b,d>0,

\frac{a+c}{b+d}.
\]

interval still contains $x$.  Then this process generates a nested
sequence of rational intervals containing $x$.

nodes.name: Arithmetic Operations on Real-Valued Functions
nodes.question: Define the pointwise sum of two real-valued functions $f,g : X \to \mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_display: $(f+g)(x) := f(x) + g(x)$ for all $x \in X$. The operations $-, \cdot, /, \max, \min, c\cdot$ are defined analogously.
nodes.statement_tex: $(f+g)(x) := f(x) + g(x)$ for all $x \in X$. The operations $-, \cdot, /, \max, \min, c\cdot$ are defined analogously.
nodes.hint: Pointwise = apply the operation value-by-value. No limits involved.
nodes.formal: Each operation is pointwise: the value of the combined function at $x$ depends only on $f(x)$ and $g(x)$, not on nearby values. Formally, $f + g$ is the unique function $h : X \to \mathbb{R}$ such that $h(x) = f(x) + g(x)$ for all $x \in X$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: Each operation is pointwise: the value of the combined function at $x$ depends only on $f(x)$ and $g(x)$, not on nearby values. Formally, $f + g$ is the unique function $h : X \to \mathbb{R}$ such that $h(x) = f(x) + g(x)$ for all $x \in X$.
nodes.negation_display: $fg$ is not composition: $(fg)(x) = f(x)g(x)$ but $(f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x))$. These agree only in special cases.

nodes.name: Stern–Brocot Limit Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Stern–Brocot Limit Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
Let

\[
\left(\frac{a_n}{b_n}\right),\quad
\left(\frac{c_n}{d_n}\right)
\]

be sequences of rationals obtained by successive Stern–Brocot
refinement so that

\[
\frac{a_n}{b_n}
<
\frac{c_n}{d_n}
\]

and

\[
\left[\frac{a_{n+1}}{b_{n+1}},\frac{c_{n+1}}{d_{n+1}}\right]
\subseteq
\left[\frac{a_n}{b_n},\frac{c_n}{d_n}\right].
\]

Then there exists a unique real number

\[
x
=
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{a_n}{b_n}
=
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{c_n}{d_n}.
\]

nodes.name: Cantor's Intersection Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Cantor's Intersection Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(X, d)$ be a metric space and let
\[
  A_1 \supseteq A_2 \supseteq A_3 \supseteq \cdots
\]
be a nested sequence of nonempty compact subsets of $X$. Then
\[
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n
\]
is compact and nonempty.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:cantors-intersection-theorem}
Let $(X, d)$ be a metric space and let
\[
  A_1 \supseteq A_2 \supseteq A_3 \supseteq \cdots
\]
be a nested sequence of nonempty compact subsets of $X$. Then
\[
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n
\]
is compact and nonempty.
nodes.formal:
$\forall (X,d)$ metric space,
$\forall (A_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ with each $A_n \subseteq X$ compact, $A_n \neq \emptyset$,
and $A_{n+1} \subseteq A_n$:
\[
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \neq \emptyset
  \quad\text{and}\quad
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \text{ is compact.}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\forall (X,d)$ metric space,
$\forall (A_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ with each $A_n \subseteq X$ compact, $A_n \neq \emptyset$,
and $A_{n+1} \subseteq A_n$:
\[
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \neq \emptyset
  \quad\text{and}\quad
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \text{ is compact.}
\]

\[
  A_1 \supseteq A_2 \supseteq A_3 \supseteq \cdots
\]

\[
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n
\]
is compact and nonempty.

\[
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \neq \emptyset
  \quad\text{and}\quad
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \text{ is compact.}
\]

nodes.name: Cancellation in Integral Domains
nodes.question: State cancellation in an integral domain.
nodes.statement_display: In integral domain $R$: $a \neq 0$ and $ab = ac \Rightarrow b = c$.
nodes.statement_tex: In integral domain $R$: $a \neq 0$ and $ab = ac \Rightarrow b = c$.
nodes.hint: $ab = ac \Rightarrow a(b-c) = 0$; since $a \neq 0$ and no zero divisors, $b - c = 0$.
nodes.negation_display: Fails in $\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$: $2 \cdot 1 = 2 \cdot 4 = 2$ but $1 \neq 4$.

nodes.proof_sketch: Cases 1, 2, 5 are direct triangle-inequality arguments. Case 3 uses the add-and-subtract decomposition $a_m b_m - a_n b_n = a_m(b_m-b_n) + b_n(a_m-a_n)$ together with the boundedness of Cauchy sequences (T6.2). Case 4 uses the uniform lower bound $|b_n| \ge \delta$ to control the denominator. No limits are named anywhere: this is an intrinsic, internal argument.
nodes.uses_text: Definition of Cauchy sequence; triangle inequality; Cauchy Sequences Are Bounded (for Case 3).

nodes.name: Cantor's Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Cantor's Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
For any set $A$, there is no surjection $A \to \mathcal{P}(A)$. In
particular, $|A| < |\mathcal{P}(A)|$.
nodes.formal: $\forall A:\; \neg\exists f: A\to\mathcal{P}(A),\; \forall S\in\mathcal{P}(A),\; \exists a\in A,\; f(a)=S$. Equivalently: $\forall f: A\to\mathcal{P}(A),\; \exists D\in\mathcal{P}(A),\; \forall a\in A,\; f(a)\neq D$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall A:\; \neg\exists f: A\to\mathcal{P}(A),\; \forall S\in\mathcal{P}(A),\; \exists a\in A,\; f(a)=S$. Equivalently: $\forall f: A\to\mathcal{P}(A),\; \exists D\in\mathcal{P}(A),\; \forall a\in A,\; f(a)\neq D$.

statement.plain:
For any set $A$, there is no surjection $A \to \mathcal{P}(A)$. In
particular, $|A| < |\mathcal{P}(A)|$.

formal.plain: $\forall A:\; \neg\exists f: A\to\mathcal{P}(A),\; \forall S\in\mathcal{P}(A),\; \exists a\in A,\; f(a)=S$. Equivalently: $\forall f: A\to\mathcal{P}(A),\; \exists D\in\mathcal{P}(A),\; \forall a\in A,\; f(a)\neq D$.

flash_a.plain:
For any set $A$, there is no surjection $A \to \mathcal{P}(A)$. In
particular, $|A| < |\mathcal{P}(A)|$.

nodes.name: Countable union of countable sets is countable
nodes.question: State the theorem: Countable union of countable sets is countable.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\{A_n\}_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ be a countable family of countable sets.
Then $\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n$ is countable.

statement.plain:
Let $\{A_n\}_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ be a countable family of countable sets.
Then $\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n$ is countable.

nodes.name: Schröder--Bernstein Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Schröder--Bernstein Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
If $|A| \leq |B|$ and $|B| \leq |A|$, then $A \sim B$.

Equivalently: if there exist injections $f : A \hookrightarrow B$ and
$g : B \hookrightarrow A$, then there exists a bijection $h : A \to B$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:schroder-bernstein}
If $|A| \leq |B|$ and $|B| \leq |A|$, then $A \sim B$.

Equivalently: if there exist injections $f : A \hookrightarrow B$ and
$g : B \hookrightarrow A$, then there exists a bijection $h : A \to B$.
nodes.formal: $\bigl(\exists f: A\hookrightarrow B\bigr) \wedge \bigl(\exists g: B\hookrightarrow A\bigr) \;\to\; \exists h: A\to B,\; h$ bijective.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\bigl(\exists f: A\hookrightarrow B\bigr) \wedge \bigl(\exists g: B\hookrightarrow A\bigr) \;\to\; \exists h: A\to B,\; h$ bijective.

statement.plain:

formal.plain: $\bigl(\exists f: A\hookrightarrow B\bigr) \wedge \bigl(\exists g: B\hookrightarrow A\bigr) \;\to\; \exists h: A\to B,\; h$ bijective.

nodes.name: Equivalence Relations and Partitions
nodes.question: State the theorem: Equivalence Relations and Partitions.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A$ be a set.
  1. If $R$ is an equivalence relation on $A$, then $A/R$ is a partition of $A$.
  2. If $\mathcal{P}$ is a partition of $A$, then the relation $R_{\mathcal{P}}$ defined by \[ (a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \exists P \in \mathcal{P} \text{ with } a \in P \text{ and } b \in P \] is an equivalence relation on $A$.
  3. These constructions are inverse: $R_{A/R} = R$ and $A/R_{\mathcal{P}} = \mathcal{P}$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:equiv-partition}
Let $A$ be a set.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item If $R$ is an equivalence relation on $A$, then $A/R$ is a partition of $A$.
\item If $\mathcal{P}$ is a partition of $A$, then the relation
$R_{\mathcal{P}}$ defined by
\[
(a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\exists P \in \mathcal{P} \text{ with } a \in P \text{ and } b \in P
\]
is an equivalence relation on $A$.
\item These constructions are inverse: $R_{A/R} = R$ and $A/R_{\mathcal{P}} = \mathcal{P}$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.formal:
(i) $\forall a \in A,\; [a]_R \neq \varnothing$; $\forall a,b \in A,\; [a]_R \neq [b]_R \to [a]_R \cap [b]_R = \varnothing$; $\bigcup_{a \in A}[a]_R = A$.\\
(ii) $\forall a,b \in A:\; (a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\leftrightarrow\; \exists P \in \mathcal{P},\; a \in P \wedge b \in P$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
(i) $\forall a \in A,\; [a]_R \neq \varnothing$; $\forall a,b \in A,\; [a]_R \neq [b]_R \to [a]_R \cap [b]_R = \varnothing$; $\bigcup_{a \in A}[a]_R = A$.\\
(ii) $\forall a,b \in A:\; (a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\leftrightarrow\; \exists P \in \mathcal{P},\; a \in P \wedge b \in P$.

statement.plain:
Let $A$ be a set.
  1. If $R$ is an equivalence relation on $A$, then $A/R$ is a partition of $A$.
  2. If $\mathcal{P}$ is a partition of $A$, then the relation $R_{\mathcal{P}}$ defined by \[ (a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \exists P \in \mathcal{P} \text{ with } a \in P \text{ and } b \in P \] is an equivalence relation on $A$.
  3. These constructions are inverse: $R_{A/R} = R$ and $A/R_{\mathcal{P}} = \mathcal{P}$.

formal.plain:
(i) $\forall a \in A,\; [a]_R \neq \varnothing$; $\forall a,b \in A,\; [a]_R \neq [b]_R \to [a]_R \cap [b]_R = \varnothing$; $\bigcup_{a \in A}[a]_R = A$.\\
(ii) $\forall a,b \in A:\; (a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\leftrightarrow\; \exists P \in \mathcal{P},\; a \in P \wedge b \in P$.

flash_q.plain: State the theorem: Equivalence Relations and Partitions.

flash_a.plain:
Let $A$ be a set.
  1. If $R$ is an equivalence relation on $A$, then $A/R$ is a partition of $A$.
  2. If $\mathcal{P}$ is a partition of $A$, then the relation $R_{\mathcal{P}}$ defined by \[ (a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \exists P \in \mathcal{P} \text{ with } a \in P \text{ and } b \in P \] is an equivalence relation on $A$.
  3. These constructions are inverse: $R_{A/R} = R$ and $A/R_{\mathcal{P}} = \mathcal{P}$.

nodes.name: Index of an Equivalence Relation
nodes.question: State the definition: Index of an Equivalence Relation.
nodes.statement_display: The index of $R$ on $A$ is the cardinality $|A/R|$, i.e.\ the number of equivalence classes.

statement.plain: The index of $R$ on $A$ is the cardinality $|A/R|$, i.e.\ the number of equivalence classes.

nodes.name: Representative Independence Lemma
nodes.question: State the lemma: Representative Independence Lemma.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. For any $a,b \in A$,
\[
[a] = [b]
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
(a,b) \in R.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{lem:rep-independence}
Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. For any $a,b \in A$,
\[
[a] = [b]
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
(a,b) \in R.
\]

statement.plain:
